component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each service card is a rectangular tile within the services list grid, visually similar to the large rectangular images in the mockup but with a text-first, corporate look. On desktop, each card occupies one grid cell: roughly 1/4 of the services column width on large screens (for four-per-row), with 16–24px of horizontal and vertical spacing between neighboring cards. The card has a flat, light background (typically white) with a subtle border or single-pixel outline and 16–24px of internal padding, in line with the content box padding from the mockup. Content inside the card is stacked top to bottom: a service title at the top, a short description or key details below, followed by a small set of metadata (e.g., location, capacity) and a clear call-to-action link or button at the bottom. Text is left-aligned, and the internal vertical spacing is moderate (~8–16px between text blocks), making the cards feel information-dense yet structured. The height of cards may vary slightly based on content, but the grid keeps their top edges aligned per row, preserving a tidy strip-like appearance across each row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rendered repeatedly inside the services list grid. Each card is a standard block-level element participating in the grid layout: it does not stick or float, but simply fills its assigned grid cell and reflows as the grid changes column count on different screen sizes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls sit at the bottom of the services list column, directly beneath the last row of service cards and still within the right-hand column of the main two-column band. There is a clear vertical separation of about 24–32px between the final row of cards and the pagination band, echoing the spacing between major sections in the mockup. The pagination elements themselves (page numbers, Previous/Next links) are horizontally centered within the services column, forming a compact row with 8–16px spacing between individual controls. The band’s height is modest, with just enough top and bottom padding (~8–16px) to visually separate it from the content above and the page edge below, acting as a clean, understated terminus to the scrolling results area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow of the right column: it appears only when there are enough results to warrant multiple pages and scrolls into view at the bottom of the results. It does not stick or float; as more cards load or pages change, it remains anchored to the end of the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When no results are found, the empty state message appears in place of the service cards within the right-hand results column, starting at the same vertical position where the first row of cards would normally appear. It is centered within this column horizontally, occupying about 60–80% of the column width, with generous internal padding of 24–32px that mirrors the spacing used in the hero and text blocks of the mockup. The message is displayed as a simple, flat panel with a short, prominent headline (e.g., “No piling services match your filters”), a one- or two-line explanatory text below, and optionally a secondary action (like a “Clear filters” link) underneath. The overall effect is a compact yet clearly delineated message block, surrounded by ample white space above and below (~24–40px) to visually emphasize the change in state and prevent it from feeling cramped next to the filters sidebar on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline replacement for the services list within the right column. It only appears when the list is empty, occupying the same structural slot. It scrolls normally with the page and does not affect the position of the filters panel on the left.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state-manager component operates behind the scenes alongside the initial state manager, conceptually layered over the entire filters-and-results band without occupying visible space. It synchronizes selected filters, search terms, and pagination with the URL but renders no UI and has no visual footprint, so it does not alter the position or sizing of any visible components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial. It is logically attached to the page root or the main piling search container, updating the URL as the user interacts, without affecting layout or flow.

name: Service Detail
title: Service Detail
description: Displays full technical details for a selected piling or earthworks service and provides a tailored quote request form for that specific service.

core_features: Load full details for a specific service using the URL parameter and the getServiceDetail interface.
Display service technical information such as category, type, descriptions, applications, depth ranges, suitable soil types, and typical durations.
Render a service-specific quote form whose selectable options (project types, soil types, timeframes) are populated from getServiceDetail.quote_form_options.
Validate and submit a tailored quote request for the current service using the submitServiceQuoteRequest interface.
Show submission feedback (success or failure) and surface the created quote request identifier returned by submitServiceQuoteRequest.
Provide a secondary navigation link to contact.html for follow-up enquiries about the same service and quote.
user_workflows:
View a specific service:
1. User clicks a service on a listing page (e.g., piling_services.html or earthworks_services.html).
2. The browser navigates to service_detail.html with a URL parameter id set to the selected Service.id.
3. On page load, the page reads the id parameter and calls getServiceDetail with that value.
4. If getServiceDetail returns data, the page displays the service name, category, descriptions, technical parameters (including depth ranges and soil suitability), applications, and typical duration.
5. If getServiceDetail fails or returns no data, the page shows an error state and prompts the user to choose another service or navigate back.

Request a tailored quote for the service:
1. After reviewing the service details, the user scrolls to the quote form on the same page.
2. The form fields (especially dropdowns for project type, soil type, and project start timeframe) are populated from getServiceDetail.quote_form_options.
3. The user enters project description, site postcode, selects project type and soil type, selects a project start timeframe, and fills in contact details and an optional budget cap.
4. On form submission, client-side validation runs (e.g., required contact details and postcode, valid email format, required option selections where applicable).
5. If validation passes, the page calls submitServiceQuoteRequest, passing the current serviceId (from the URL id), all form field values, and leaving any optional fields empty if the user did not provide them.
6. The page receives the response (including quote_request_id, status, success flag, and message) and updates the UI: if successful, it displays a success confirmation with the quote request identifier; if not, it displays an error message and keeps the form data for correction.
7. Optionally, the page updates the URL query string with a flag and/or the returned quote_request_id so that the success state is preserved on refresh.

Follow up via the contact page:
1. After submitting a quote (or if the user prefers a general discussion), the user clicks the secondary link such as "Contact us about this service".
2. The page navigates to contact.html, appending the enquiry_type and subject parameters specified for this navigation (enquiry_type set to quote_enquiry and subject set to a follow-up description).
3. The contact page reads these parameters to preconfigure its own form (e.g., selecting the appropriate enquiry type and subject).
interactions: On page load, read the URL parameter id. If present, call getServiceDetail with this value and show a loading state until the response is received.

If getServiceDetail resolves successfully, map the returned fields into read-only sections: summary (name, category_name, service_type, short_description), technical specifications (pile_type, min_recommended_depth_m, max_recommended_depth_m, suitable_soil_types, typical_duration_description), and applications (typical_applications, project_types).
If getServiceDetail fails (network error, invalid id, or missing data), show an error message and hide or disable the quote form to prevent submissions without a valid service context.

Populate the quote form’s selectable fields (project_type, soil_type, project_start_timeframe) from getServiceDetail.quote_form_options, binding each dropdown’s options directly to the corresponding array in the response.
As the user types or selects values in the quote form, maintain an in-memory form state object that mirrors the parameters required by submitServiceQuoteRequest.
On quote form submission, run client-side validation; if invalid, prevent the API call and display inline validation messages for the relevant fields.
If validation passes, call submitServiceQuoteRequest with the current serviceId and the values from the form state; disable the submit button and show a submitting state until the response arrives.

When submitServiceQuoteRequest returns, inspect the success flag: if true, show a success feedback component that includes the quote_request_id and message; if false, show an error feedback component with the message and allow the user to retry.

After a successful submission, update the browser URL (for example via history.replaceState) to include URL parameters representing the submission state (such as a flag indicating that a quote has been submitted and the associated quote_request_id), so that reloading the page re-displays the success state without re-submitting the form.

When the user clicks the "Contact us about this service" link, navigate to contact.html with the configured enquiry_type and subject URL parameters, and optionally include the current service id and/or quote request id as additional parameters for context.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver for this page is the URL parameter id, which must correspond to a Service.id. On initial load and whenever the id parameter changes (for example, if the user navigates directly to another service by changing the URL), the page calls getServiceDetail with that value to refresh all displayed service information and the quote form options. The existence and validity of id determines whether the page is in a normal display state or an error state (when id is missing or getServiceDetail fails). Additional optional URL parameters may represent transient interaction state, such as whether a quote has recently been submitted and the identifier of that quote request. These parameters are updated after a successful submitServiceQuoteRequest call (e.g., using history.replaceState) and are read on subsequent loads to restore the submission feedback (success banner and quote_request_id) without re-calling submitServiceQuoteRequest. All other state, such as the current in-progress form input values, is held in memory and reset on full page reloads; no localStorage or similar persistent client storage is used.

components.functionality: Reads the service ID from the URL, invokes getServiceDetail, and manages loading, success, and error states for the Service data used across the page.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Read URL parameter id and, if present, call getServiceDetail with this value.
onUrlParamChange: When the id parameter changes, re-call getServiceDetail to refresh the page data.
onLoadSuccess: Store the getServiceDetail response in a shared state object (including service fields and quote_form_options) and set a ready state for dependent components.
onLoadError: Record the error, set an error state flag, and notify dependent components to show an error message and disable the quote form.

components.functionality: Displays the main identifying information for the current service, including its name, category label, service type, and short description, to give users a quick understanding of what the service covers.
event_handlers: onDataBound: When new Service data is available from service-detail-loader, render the name, category_name, service_type, and short_description fields.
onErrorStateChange: If the loader indicates an error state, hide this section or replace it with a message indicating that the service could not be loaded.

components.functionality: Shows key technical parameters of the service such as pile type (if applicable), recommended depth range, suitable soil types, and typical duration information, providing engineers or project managers with technical suitability insight.
event_handlers: onDataBound: Render pile_type, min_recommended_depth_m, max_recommended_depth_m, suitable_soil_types, and typical_duration_description from the Service data when available.

onConditionalDisplay: Hide or adjust subsections if certain fields are absent or not relevant for the current service (for example, skip pile-specific attributes when the service_type is not a piling service).

components.functionality: Displays typical applications and associated project types for the service to help users understand where and how the service is commonly used.
event_handlers: onDataBound: Present typical_applications and project_types from the Service data in a structured list or grouped format.

onOptionSync: Optionally cross-reference Service.project_types with the quote_form_options.project_types (through shared state) so the text in this section aligns with the options available in the quote form.

components.functionality: Provides a tailored quote request form for the current service, preconfigured with service-specific project type, soil type, and timeframe options, and submits a QuoteRequest via the submitServiceQuoteRequest interface.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize an in-memory form state object with empty fields mapped to the parameters expected by submitServiceQuoteRequest (project_description, site_postcode, project_type, soil_type, project_start_timeframe, contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, budget_cap).

onOptionsBind: Populate dropdown/select controls for project_type, soil_type, and project_start_timeframe from the quote_form_options.project_types, quote_form_options.soil_types, and quote_form_options.project_start_timeframes arrays provided by getServiceDetail.
onFieldChange: Update the form state whenever the user types or changes any field, and clear any validation messages for that field.

onValidate: Before submission, check required fields (such as site_postcode, at least one contact field, and any required selection fields) and verify basic formats (e.g., email syntax). If validation fails, prevent submission and attach validation messages to relevant fields.

onSubmit: If validation passes, call submitServiceQuoteRequest with the current serviceId from the URL and all values from the form state, set a submitting flag, and temporarily disable further submissions.

onSubmitSuccess: When submitServiceQuoteRequest returns with success=true, read quote_request_id, status, and message, update a submission result state object shared with the feedback component, optionally clear or lock the form, and request a URL update (e.g., to store the quote_request_id and submission indicator in query parameters).

onSubmitError: When submitServiceQuoteRequest returns with success=false or an error occurs, display the error message in-context, re-enable the submit button, and keep the user’s form input so they can correct and resubmit.

components.functionality: Presents the outcome of the latest quote submission attempt, including success confirmation with the quote request identifier or error messages prompting the user to retry.
event_handlers: onResultChange: When the service-quote-form updates the submission result state (e.g., after submitServiceQuoteRequest resolves), render a corresponding success or error banner including the message and, on success, the quote_request_id.

onUrlStateInit: On page load, inspect URL parameters for any persisted submission state (such as an indicator that a quote has been submitted and an associated quote_request_id) and reconstruct the feedback display accordingly without re-submitting.
onDismiss: If the user closes the feedback banner, hide it and optionally update the URL to remove submission-related parameters.

components.functionality: Provides a secondary action allowing the user to navigate to the general contact page for follow-up about the current service and any related quote request.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate to contact.html with URL parameters enquiry_type set to quote_enquiry and subject set to a follow-up description as specified in the page architecture; optionally include additional contextual URL parameters such as the current service id and the latest quote_request_id from the submission feedback state.

components.functionality: Handles cases where no valid service can be loaded (for example, missing or invalid id parameter or a failed getServiceDetail response) by informing the user and preventing quote submissions without a valid context.
event_handlers: onLoadError: Triggered by service-detail-loader when getServiceDetail fails; shows an error message that the service details cannot be displayed.
onHideDependentComponents: When active, signal other components (such as service-specifications-panel and service-quote-form) to hide or disable themselves to maintain data consistency.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop-oriented grid typical of classic WordPress themes, with the main content constrained to a centered max-width container and internal content (like the 4 images) snapping to equal fractional columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s stacked band pattern: global logo/contact header at the very top, then the dark full-width navigation bar. Below that, the Service Detail page content sits inside a centered, max-width container. Within this container, components are arranged top-to-bottom in clearly separated horizontal bands: first the service-summary-section acting as the hero/header for the service, followed by a two-column section pairing the service-specifications-panel on the left with the service-applications-section on the right. Beneath these details sits a full-width quote band containing the quote-submission-feedback (when present), followed immediately by the service-quote-form and the service-contact-link. The service-detail-loader and service-load-error-state occupy the same main-content region, temporarily replacing these bands when loading or error states occur.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s stacked band pattern: global logo/contact header at the very top, then the dark full-width navigation bar. Below that, the Service Detail page content sits inside a centered, max-width container. Within this container, components are arranged top-to-bottom in clearly separated horizontal bands: first the service-summary-section acting as the hero/header for the service, followed by a two-column section pairing the service-specifications-panel on the left with the service-applications-section on the right. Beneath these details sits a full-width quote band containing the quote-submission-feedback (when present), followed immediately by the service-quote-form and the service-contact-link. The service-detail-loader and service-load-error-state occupy the same main-content region, temporarily replacing these bands when loading or error states occur.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertical space mimics the mockup: the service-summary-section occupies a moderate area with generous top/bottom padding, similar to the H1 + subheading band. The two-column specifications/applications region occupies a deeper slice, reflecting the denser lower text area in the mockup. The quote form occupies another substantial band with slightly more vertical padding to support form usability. Within the main content container, width is constrained to about 70–80% of the viewport (e.g., 1040–1200px max), centered. The specifications and applications columns follow a 12-column grid: specifications-panel uses roughly 4/12 (about 30–35% of the container width) and applications-section uses 8/12 (about 65–70%). All elements respect the 8px-based spacing scale: 16–24px padding inside components, 24–40px margins between bands.
layout_strategies.content_density: The service-summary-section is relatively open, mirroring the hero’s comfortable white space: larger type, more breathing room, and minimal text. Moving downward, the service-specifications-panel and service-applications-section become more text-dense with bullet lists and technical details similar to the SERVICE vs FEATURES & APPLICATIONS columns in the mockup. The quote form is moderately dense: multiple fields arranged in a structured, grid-like layout but with enough padding to avoid feeling cramped. Loader and error states are sparse and centered, with minimal text and generous negative space for clarity. Overall, density increases gradually from top (summary) to middle (details) then stabilizes around a structured, moderate density in the form.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows the mockup’s strong vertical rhythm: users begin at the global header and navigation, then move down to the service-summary-section (service name and key summary). From there, the eye drops to the two-column band where attention starts in the left, narrower service-specifications-panel (like the SERVICE column) and then flows right to the broader service-applications-section (like FEATURES & APPLICATIONS). After scanning these lists, the eye naturally continues downward to the quote-submission-feedback banner (when visible) and then into the service-quote-form as the key conversion area. The service-contact-link is placed adjacent to the form header or footer as a secondary, right-aligned text link, offering an alternate path. Loader and error states interrupt this flow in the same region but maintain the centered, banded layout so the overall page structure remains consistent.

layout.overall_description: The Service Detail page sits beneath the existing corporate header and dark navigation bar, continuing the conservative, content-first WordPress-style layout. Below the nav, a centered content container spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, with clean left/right margins. At the top of this container, the service-summary-section forms a hero-like band: the service title appears left-aligned as a prominent H1 with a concise subheading beneath it, both surrounded by comfortable white space. A subtle horizontal rule runs the full width of the content container below the summary, visually echoing the mockup and separating the hero text from the main body. Under this rule, the page transitions into a denser two-column region: on the left, a narrower column houses the service-specifications-panel; on the right, a wider column holds the service-applications-section, both using bullet lists that read much like the SERVICE and FEATURES & APPLICATIONS columns in the mockup. Margins between rows of text keep the band readable while still packing in information. Below this detailed band, a full-width section holds the quoting experience: first, a low-height status stripe (quote-submission-feedback) that appears only when needed, followed immediately by the service-quote-form occupying the full width of the content container, with form fields arranged in a simple grid. Near the top-right or bottom-right of this form block, the service-contact-link appears as a secondary text-based call-to-action. When the page is loading or encounters an error, the usual content bands in the container are temporarily replaced with a single, centered loader or error panel, but the overall page still maintains the same top-aligned header and nav, preserving the familiar vertical flow. The entire layout feels straightforward and functional—stacked horizontal bands, modest spacing, and clearly structured columns—closely mirroring the slightly dated but practical business theme aesthetic described in the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader occupies the primary content region directly beneath the global navigation bar, inside the same centered max-width container used by the rest of the page. When active, it effectively replaces all other service-detail components. Vertically, it sits approximately 24–32px below the nav bar, matching the top padding used for the service-summary-section. The loader content (spinner or skeleton) is centered horizontally within the container and vertically within an area that takes up about 30–40% of the viewport height, leaving generous white space above and below. If using a skeleton layout, the shapes loosely mimic the final structure: a wide bar across the top where the H1 would be, a few shorter bars beneath, then two side-by-side rectangular blocks where the specifications and applications columns would sit, and a wider block representing the quote form. This preserves the sense of banding and columns even during loading, consistent with the mockup’s structured grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the normal document flow, but conditionally rendered so that when active it is the only visible component within the main content container (summary, details, and form are hidden). It scrolls with the page like any other block-level section, though its content is typically short enough that scrolling is not required.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-summary-section is the first visible section within the main content container under the global header and nav. It is centered within the page width but its contents are left-aligned, reflecting the mockup’s H1 hero treatment. This block begins roughly 24–32px below the navigation bar and spans the full width of the container (about 70–80% of the viewport, max-width around 1040–1200px). Inside, the H1 service title sits flush with the left edge of the content column, with about 16–24px of padding above and below. A subheading or short descriptive paragraph is placed directly below the H1, aligned to the same left edge and limited to roughly 70% of the container width for comfortable line lengths. The vertical height of this section is moderate—around 15–20% of the viewport height on desktop—offering enough white space to feel open but not oversized. At the bottom of this section, a subtle horizontal rule runs from the left edge to the right edge of the content container, providing a clear visual break before the more structured two-column content below, matching the mockup’s hero-to-body transition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section within the vertical flow. It expands in height as needed for longer subheadings but retains consistent top/bottom padding. No sticky or floating behavior; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-specifications-panel sits immediately below the horizontal rule from the service-summary-section, forming the left column of a two-column band. This band begins about 24px below the rule. Within the shared 12-column grid of the main content container, the specifications panel occupies approximately 4 of 12 columns (about 30–35% of the container width), anchored flush to the left edge. It extends vertically to fit its bullet lists and technical details. The panel’s title (e.g., “Specifications” or similar) appears at the top, aligned left, with about 8–16px of vertical space above it. Below the title, the content is presented as a vertically stacked list, using bullets or tight paragraphs, with 8px spacing between lines and 12–16px between groups, reflecting the slightly dense but readable text layout of the SERVICE column in the mockup. Internal padding of 16–24px on all sides ensures the text doesn’t touch the column edges, though backgrounds remain largely flat and unembellished. The bottom edge of this panel lines up roughly with the bottom of the adjacent applications section on the right, so the entire two-column band reads as a coherent horizontal stripe.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static column within a responsive two-column row. On narrow viewports (if considered), it would stack above the applications section, but on the desktop-oriented layout it remains a fixed-width left column. No sticky behavior; it scrolls with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-applications-section shares a row with the service-specifications-panel, forming the wider right column of the same horizontal band. It is positioned directly to the right of the specifications panel, starting about 16–24px away from it as a gutter. Within the 12-column grid, it occupies approximately 8 of 12 columns (about 65–70% of the container width). The section title (e.g., “Features & Applications” or similar) is aligned to its own left edge, which is visually just right of the midline, mirroring the mockup’s broader text column. Below the title, bullet lists or grouped items are arranged vertically with modest spacing (around 8px between lines and 16px between section groups). The text density here is higher than in the summary but balanced by consistent line height and margins. The internal padding mirrors the specifications panel (16–24px on all sides), so the two panels feel related. Vertically, this section typically extends slightly deeper than the left column to accommodate more content, but where possible, bottom margins are adjusted so the visual bottom of both columns aligns, keeping the overall band looking like a unified strip of information, similar to the SERVICE vs FEATURES & APPLICATIONS pair in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static right-hand column within the same row as the specifications panel. It expands vertically based on content. On smaller breakpoints it would stack below the specifications panel, but in the primary desktop layout it remains side-by-side. It has no fixed or sticky behavior and scrolls with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-quote-form is placed directly beneath the two-column specifications/applications band, separated from it by a clear vertical gap of about 32–40px to establish it as a distinct next step. It occupies the full width of the main content container (all 12 columns), forming a new horizontal band similar in prominence to the image strip band in the mockup, but filled with form fields instead of images. The form section begins with a concise heading or label (e.g., “Request a Quote”) left-aligned at the top, with about 16px of top padding. Below the heading, fields are laid out in a simple two-column form grid where appropriate: for example, name and phone might be on one row (each taking 6 of 12 columns), followed by email and company on the next row, and full-width fields (like project details) spanning all 12 columns beneath. Vertical spacing between rows is around 16–24px, maintaining moderate density. The entire form has internal padding of 24–32px, giving it a slightly more substantial feel than the text sections above. This block extends vertically to accommodate all inputs and the submit button; the submit button is typically left-aligned near the bottom, with about 16–24px of clear space above it. This positioning reinforces the page’s conversion funnel: after absorbing technical details, the user encounters a clearly delineated form band aligned with the overall corporate aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the vertical flow. It occupies its own horizontal band and scrolls normally. It does not float or stick, ensuring the page retains its straightforward, document-like reading pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-submission-feedback appears as a slim, full-width banner located inside the main content container, directly above the service-quote-form. When visible, it occupies a shallow horizontal band—roughly 48–64px in height—separated from the two-column specifications/applications band by about 16–24px, and from the top of the form by about 8–16px. It spans the full width of the container (all 12 columns), aligning with the left and right edges of the form so they feel visually connected. The message text is left-aligned and vertically centered within the banner, using one or two short lines. Background color is slightly tinted (e.g., soft green for success, muted red/orange for error) but still flat and conservative, avoiding gradients or heavy shadows. This makes the banner noticeable yet unobtrusive, fitting the corporate style. When not needed, the banner’s space collapses entirely so that the form moves up to sit closer beneath the details band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered inline block. It appears and disappears within the normal document flow without affecting the layout beyond shifting the form slightly up or down. It does not overlay content and scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-contact-link is positioned as a secondary, text-based call-to-action associated with the service-quote-form. Within the quote form band, it appears either in the top-right area of the form header row or in the bottom-right area near the submit button, depending on final content, but always aligned to the right edge of the main content container. Horizontally, it sits flush with the right margin of the form fields, about 16–24px away from the container’s right edge. Vertically, when placed near the form heading, it aligns baseline with the heading; when near the submit button, it aligns vertically with the button’s midline, with 8–12px of space separating it from the button. Its visual style echoes navigation links in the dark nav bar but adapted to a light background: a simple, flat text link with slightly bolder weight or an accompanying small icon (e.g., phone), minimal decoration, and about 8px of clickable padding around the text. This placement ensures it’s easy to spot as an alternate contact method without competing visually with the primary submit button.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline or inline-block element aligned right within the quote form’s internal layout (e.g., using a right-aligned column or flex container). It remains within the form band and scrolls with it. No sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-load-error-state occupies the same main content container as the loader and standard content, appearing when service data fails to load. It begins at the same vertical offset as the service-summary-section would (about 24–32px below the nav bar) but displays as a single, prominent panel centered within the container. Horizontally, it spans approximately 60–70% of the container width (not full width), leaving a bit more whitespace on the sides than regular content to emphasize the error state. This panel has 24–32px of internal padding and a subtle border or background tint (e.g., light red or warm grey) to distinguish it, while still maintaining the flat, conservative look. The error heading is left-aligned at the top of the panel, followed by a short explanatory sentence and any retry or contact actions. Vertical spacing between these lines is 8–16px. The panel itself occupies roughly 20–30% of the viewport height, leaving ample white space above and below so it feels like a standalone band, similar in weight to the hero section but clearly styled as an alert. No other service-detail components are rendered when this error state is active, keeping the layout simple and focused.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the content container that replaces normal content upon error. It scrolls with the page, though its limited height usually means no additional scrolling is required. No overlay; it is integrated into the normal vertical flow.

name: Earthworks Services
title: Earthworks Services
description: Page for viewing and filtering earthworks-related services, with navigation to detailed service information and a custom earthworks package configuration tool.

core_features: Retrieve and display available earthworks project-type filter options using getEarthworksServiceFilterOptions.
Display a paginated list of earthworks services using listEarthworksServices, with optional filtering by project_type.
Allow users to change the project_type filter, which updates the URL and reloads the service list.
Allow users to change the page of results via pagination controls, which updates the URL and reloads the service list.
Provide a prominent action to navigate to the custom earthworks package configurator (earthworks_configurator.html).
Allow navigation to individual service detail pages (service_detail.html) when a user selects a service.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Landing on Earthworks Services with or without filters
1. User navigates to earthworks_services.html, possibly with URL parameters project_type and/or page.
2. The page controller reads project_type and page from the URL.
3. The page calls getEarthworksServiceFilterOptions to load available project-type filter options.
4. The page calls listEarthworksServices with the current project_type (if any), page (or a default page index if missing), and a configured page_size.
5. While data is loading, a loading indicator is shown for the filter options and service list.
6. When responses return, the filter component displays the project-type options with the current selection (if any), and the service list displays the services and pagination based on total_results and the current page.

Workflow: Filtering services by project type
1. User interacts with the project-type filter (e.g., selects or changes a filter option).
2. The filter component updates the URL project_type parameter to the selected value (or removes it if no filter is selected) and resets the page parameter to the first page index.
3. The page controller detects the URL change and re-invokes listEarthworksServices with the updated project_type and page.
4. The service list updates to show only services matching the selected project_type.
5. Pagination controls are updated according to the new total_results and current page.

Workflow: Changing pages in the service list
1. User clicks a pagination control (e.g., a specific page number, next, or previous action).
2. The pagination component calculates the new page index and updates the page parameter in the URL while keeping the current project_type value (if any).
3. The page controller detects the URL change and re-invokes listEarthworksServices with the current project_type and updated page.
4. The service list updates to show the services for the selected page.
5. The pagination controls update their current/active page state based on the new page parameter.

Workflow: Navigating to a service detail page
1. User clicks on a service card title or a dedicated "View Details" action within a service listing.
2. The service list component reads the id of the selected service from the listEarthworksServices response.
3. The browser navigates to service_detail.html with the URL parameter id set to the selected service.id.
4. The Earthworks Services page has no further responsibilities after navigation.

Workflow: Starting a custom earthworks package configuration
1. User clicks the "Build Your Package" / "Custom Earthworks" button.
2. The page triggers navigation to earthworks_configurator.html with no additional parameters required from this page.
3. The configurator page then handles package configuration independently; the Earthworks Services page does not persist any configuration state.

Workflow: Handling empty or error states for service listing
1. The page invokes listEarthworksServices with the current URL parameters.
2. If listEarthworksServices returns successfully but total_results indicates no services, the service list component shows an empty state message and continues to display filter controls so the user can adjust filters.
3. If the call to listEarthworksServices or getEarthworksServiceFilterOptions fails, an error state component displays a functional error message and can offer a manual retry action that re-triggers the failed call while retaining current URL parameters.
interactions:
Interaction: Project-type filter selection
- Component: project-type-filter-bar
- Trigger: User selects a project-type option (e.g., via dropdown, radio, or pill button).
- System response: Updates the URL project_type parameter to the selected option's value (or removes it if the selection is cleared), resets page to the first page, and re-fetches services via listEarthworksServices.

Interaction: Pagination click
- Component: service-list-pagination
- Trigger: User clicks a pagination control to move to a specific page or navigate to next/previous.
- System response: Updates the URL page parameter to the selected page index, preserves project_type, and re-fetches services via listEarthworksServices.

Interaction: Click service card title or details action
- Component: earthworks-service-list
- Trigger: User clicks on a service card title or a "View Details" action.
- System response: Navigates to service_detail.html with the URL parameter id set to the selected service's id value from listEarthworksServices.

Interaction: Click "Build Your Package" / "Custom Earthworks" button
- Component: earthworks-package-cta
- Trigger: User clicks the CTA button.
- System response: Navigates to earthworks_configurator.html (no additional parameters required).

Interaction: Retry loading after error
- Component: data-error-banner
- Trigger: User clicks a "Retry" action after an error is shown for filters or services.
- System response: Re-invokes the respective interface (getEarthworksServiceFilterOptions or listEarthworksServices) using the current URL parameters for project_type and page.

Interaction: Manual URL adjustment (direct link or browser navigation)
- Component: page-url-state-controller
- Trigger: User lands on the page via an external link containing project_type and/or page, or uses browser back/forward which changes the URL.
- System response: Detects URL changes, re-computes current project_type and page state, and re-fetches filters (if not already loaded) and services accordingly.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is controlled solely through URL parameters and interface responses. URL Parameters: project_type (string, optional) and page (number, optional) are read on page load and whenever they change (including browser navigation). If project_type is present, it is passed directly to listEarthworksServices as the project_type parameter; if it is absent, listEarthworksServices is called without a project_type filter so that all relevant earthworks services are considered. If page is present and valid, it is passed to listEarthworksServices as the page parameter; if it is missing or invalid, a default first-page index is used internally (without writing that default back to the URL unless triggered by a user interaction such as changing filters or pagination). The page_size parameter passed to listEarthworksServices is determined by application configuration outside this specification and is not hardcoded here. When a user selects or changes a project-type filter, the page updates the URL to set project_type to the selected value (or remove it if the user clears the filter) and sets page to the first page index; this URL update triggers a new call to listEarthworksServices. When a user changes pages via pagination, the page updates the URL page parameter while preserving the current project_type; this also triggers a new call to listEarthworksServices. Navigation to service_detail.html and earthworks_configurator.html is done via URL changes (e.g., service_detail.html?id={service.id} and earthworks_configurator.html) but those target pages manage their own state. No persistent state (such as filter selections or pagination) is stored outside of the URL and in-memory state; localStorage or other client persistence is not used.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and subsequent updates based on URL parameters. On load and on URL change, it reads project_type and page from the URL, determines the effective filter and page index, and calls getEarthworksServiceFilterOptions and listEarthworksServices as required. It passes the resulting data to child components and listens for events from those components that require URL updates.
event_handlers: onInit: Called when the page is first loaded; reads URL parameters, calls getEarthworksServiceFilterOptions and listEarthworksServices with current project_type and page, and populates child components.
onUrlChange: Called when the browser URL changes due to back/forward navigation or internal updates; re-reads project_type and page and re-invokes listEarthworksServices with the new parameters.
onFilterChange: Triggered by project-type-filter-bar when the user selects or clears a filter; updates URL project_type and resets page, then calls listEarthworksServices with updated parameters.
onPageChange: Triggered by service-list-pagination when the user selects a different page; updates URL page while preserving project_type, then calls listEarthworksServices with updated parameters.
onRetryFiltersLoad: Triggered by data-error-banner when a filter-loading error is retried; calls getEarthworksServiceFilterOptions again.
onRetryServicesLoad: Triggered by data-error-banner when a service-listing error is retried; calls listEarthworksServices again using the current project_type and page.

components.functionality: Displays the available earthworks project-type filters and allows the user to select or clear a project-type filter. Reflects the current selection based on the URL project_type parameter and notifies the page controller when the selection changes.
event_handlers: onFilterOptionSelect: Invoked when the user selects a specific project-type option; emits an event with the selected option value to the page-url-state-controller (onFilterChange).
onFilterClear: Invoked when the user clears the project-type filter; emits an event indicating that project_type should be removed from the URL.
onFiltersLoaded: Invoked after getEarthworksServiceFilterOptions data is bound; synchronizes the visual selected state to match the project_type value currently in the URL.

components.functionality: Renders the list of earthworks services returned by listEarthworksServices, including key information from each service object. Each service item is interactive, allowing navigation to a detailed service page. The component also shows loading, empty, and error states based on data loading status and list content.
event_handlers: onServiceClick: Invoked when the user clicks a service card title or its 'View Details' action; navigates the browser to service_detail.html with id set to the clicked service's id.

onServicesLoading: Invoked by the page-url-state-controller when a new request to listEarthworksServices is initiated; shows the services-loading-indicator and hides current results until the response arrives or is merged.
onServicesLoaded: Invoked when listEarthworksServices returns successfully; updates the displayed list and evaluates whether an empty state should be displayed when there are no services.

onServicesLoadError: Invoked when listEarthworksServices fails; displays the data-error-banner specific to the service list and offers a retry that triggers onRetryServicesLoad in the page-url-state-controller.

components.functionality: Provides controls to navigate between pages of the service list. Calculates the number of pages based on total_results and page_size, tracks the current page from the URL, and emits page-change events when the user selects a new page.
event_handlers: onPageSelect: Invoked when the user clicks a specific page number or a next/previous control; calculates the target page index and dispatches onPageChange to the page-url-state-controller.
onPaginationStateSync: Invoked after each call to listEarthworksServices to synchronize the active page indicator with the current page parameter from the URL.

onDisableControlsConditionally: Evaluates whether to disable previous/next controls when the current page is at the beginning or end of the available pages, using listEarthworksServices.total_results and the configured page_size.

components.functionality: Displays the 'Build Your Package' / 'Custom Earthworks' primary action and navigates the user to the earthworks package configurator page when clicked.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigates the browser to earthworks_configurator.html when the user activates the button.

components.functionality: Shows a loading state for the services list whenever listEarthworksServices is in progress, and hides when loading completes or errors.
event_handlers: onServicesLoadingStart: Triggered by page-url-state-controller before calling listEarthworksServices; displays the loading indicator.
onServicesLoadingEnd: Triggered after listEarthworksServices completes successfully or with an error; hides the loading indicator.

components.functionality: Shows a loading state for the project-type filter options whenever getEarthworksServiceFilterOptions is in progress, and hides when loading completes or errors.
event_handlers: onFiltersLoadingStart: Triggered by page-url-state-controller before calling getEarthworksServiceFilterOptions; displays the loading indicator.
onFiltersLoadingEnd: Triggered after getEarthworksServiceFilterOptions completes successfully or with an error; hides the loading indicator.

components.functionality: Displays a functional message when listEarthworksServices returns successfully but contains no services. Encourages the user to adjust filters or use the package configurator CTA.
event_handlers: onServicesLoaded: Checks if total_results indicates an empty result set; if so, renders the empty-state message and hides the standard service list rendering.
onFilterChange: Hides the empty-state message when a new filter selection triggers a new data load, until the new results arrive.

components.functionality: Displays functional error messages when either getEarthworksServiceFilterOptions or listEarthworksServices fails. Provides retry actions that call back into the page controller to reattempt the failed operations using the current URL state.
event_handlers: onFiltersLoadError: Triggered when getEarthworksServiceFilterOptions fails; shows an error banner related to filters and displays a retry button that, when clicked, triggers onRetryFiltersLoad in the page-url-state-controller.

onServicesLoadError: Triggered when listEarthworksServices fails; shows an error banner related to service listings and displays a retry button that, when clicked, triggers onRetryServicesLoad in the page-url-state-controller.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop-oriented grid typical of classic WordPress themes, with the main content constrained to a centered max-width container and internal content (like the 4 images) snapping to equal fractional columns.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a strictly top‑to‑bottom, banded layout inside a centered max‑width content container beneath the site header and nav. Start with the page title and short explanatory copy (hero text), followed by a subtle divider line. Directly beneath that, place a full‑width filter bar as its own horizontal band. Below the filters, dedicate the main body band to the earthworks service list in a multi‑column grid (visually echoing the four‑image strip from the mockup). Under the grid, center the pagination controls, then finish with a lower CTA band that encourages users into the package configuration tool. Error, loading, and empty states appear in place of or on top of their respective bands without breaking the overall vertical order.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a strictly top‑to‑bottom, banded layout inside a centered max‑width content container beneath the site header and nav. Start with the page title and short explanatory copy (hero text), followed by a subtle divider line. Directly beneath that, place a full‑width filter bar as its own horizontal band. Below the filters, dedicate the main body band to the earthworks service list in a multi‑column grid (visually echoing the four‑image strip from the mockup). Under the grid, center the pagination controls, then finish with a lower CTA band that encourages users into the package configuration tool. Error, loading, and empty states appear in place of or on top of their respective bands without breaking the overall vertical order.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Constrain all page components (except the global header/nav) to a centered container spanning roughly 80–90% of the viewport width, aligned to a 12‑column grid. The hero text takes a moderate vertical slice with comfortable padding (24–40px above and below). The filter bar is relatively compact but clearly separated with around 16–24px vertical spacing above and below. The service grid occupies the largest vertical region, with multiple rows of cards and 16–24px gaps. Pagination is compact with about 24px top margin. The final CTA band has a bit more breathing room (32–40px top and bottom) to stand out, similar to a capped section at the bottom of the page. Error banners are shallow horizontal strips; loading and empty states reuse the height of their parent bands.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep the hero area and CTA band open and readable with generous padding and limited text. Increase density in the filter bar and service grid, where interactive controls and multiple service cards sit closer together, following the moderately dense lower sections of the mockup. Service cards are arranged tightly side‑by‑side with narrow gutters to maximize visible options per row, mimicking the four image thumbnails. Lists within cards can be moderately dense but must retain at least 16px vertical spacing between items. System states (loading/empty/error) simplify the content temporarily, reducing perceived density and drawing attention to the status messaging.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves down from the global header/nav into the left‑aligned page title and subheading, then to a light rule that visually caps the hero. From there, attention drops immediately to the filter bar as the first interactive control. After filters are set, the gaze moves down into the service grid, scanning rows from left to right similar to the four‑image row from the mockup. At the bottom of the grid, the eye naturally encounters pagination centered in the column, reinforcing continuation controls. Finally, the user reaches the CTA band that anchors the bottom of the page, offering a clear next step. Error and loading banners appear near the top of the content stack so they are seen before or instead of the main content; the empty state appears in the grid area, ensuring the user understands that filters are responsible for the lack of results.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the site’s existing logo/contact header and dark full‑width navigation bar, the Earthworks Services page content sits inside a centered, max‑width container that feels like a classic business WordPress layout. At the very top of this container, any error banner forms a shallow, full‑width strip, followed by a left‑aligned H1 title and short description spaced comfortably with generous top and bottom padding. A thin horizontal rule runs across the content width, marking the end of the hero band. Immediately under this rule, a compact but clearly separated filter bar spans the full container width, presenting project‑type filters aligned horizontally against a subtly tinted background. Below this, the main visual bulk of the page is a 3–4‑column service grid, where rectangular service cards sit in tight rows reminiscent of the four machinery images from the mockup. When services are loading or empty, that same region is occupied by status messaging centered within the grid area. Under the grid, pagination controls are centered and lightly spaced, forming a narrow control band. Finally, a bottom CTA section stretches across the container with extra padding, a clear headline, supporting line, and a strong button aligned to the left, capping the page in a way that feels consistent with the conservative, banded layout: functional, content‑first, and slightly traditional rather than ultra‑modern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non‑visual, structural component with no explicit footprint in the visible layout. It resides logically at the top of the page content architecture, just under the global navigation in the DOM, orchestrating which services are shown and how filters map to the URL. It does not occupy any width or height, nor does it alter spacing between visible bands; all visual components are laid out as if this controller were not present on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non‑rendered; participates only in routing/state management with no spatial behavior in the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project‑type filter bar forms its own horizontal band directly beneath the hero text and the thin horizontal rule, inside the main content container. It spans the full width of that container (roughly 80–90% of the viewport, centered) and aligns to the same 12‑column grid as the service list below. Vertically, it is compact but clearly defined, with about 16–24px of internal padding top and bottom. Filter controls (dropdowns, pill buttons, icons) are arranged primarily in a single horizontal row starting from the left, with a small 16px gap between controls, allowing the bar to feel functional and slightly dense, similar in weight to the navigation bar but lighter in color. There is approximately 24px of vertical space separating the bottom of the rule above and the top of the filter bar, and another 24px margin below it before the service grid begins, reinforcing its identity as a separate band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element in the vertical flow; scrolls away with the page and does not stick or float. On narrower widths, its controls may wrap to multiple lines within the same band while maintaining the same left alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The earthworks service list occupies the largest central band of the page, immediately below the filter bar, within the same centered content container. It uses the implied 12‑column grid to form a multi‑column layout of rectangular cards. On desktop, cards are sized to visually echo the four‑image row from the mockup: typically four cards per row (each roughly 3 columns wide) with narrow 16–24px gutters between them, so the grid stretches nearly the full width of the container. Each card has internal padding of about 16–24px and a simple, flat appearance with minimal decoration, consistent with the older corporate theme style. Vertically, cards stack in rows with around 24px spacing between rows, creating a moderately dense but legible grid. There is about 24px of top margin between the filter bar and the first row of cards, and around 24–32px of space between the last row of cards and the pagination controls below. When populated, this band dominates the middle of the page, drawing the eye in a left‑to‑right, then downward scanning pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block layout within the container; grid items wrap naturally to the next line based on available width. The overall grid region expands vertically as more services load, always pushing pagination and the CTA downward in the vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls form a compact horizontal strip directly beneath the earthworks service list, within the same centered container. They are horizontally centered relative to the content column, occupying only the space needed for page numbers and navigation arrows, leaving ample whitespace on either side. There is approximately 24–32px of vertical margin above the pagination (separating it from the last row of cards) and about 24–32px margin below it before the bottom CTA band, giving it a distinct but unobtrusive presence. The controls are arranged in a single line, with 8–16px spacing between page numbers and next/previous icons, mirroring the conservative, functional style of the site: text‑based links or simple outlined buttons rather than large graphical elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline-block elements centered within a standard block container; scrolls with the content and remains in the vertical flow, always appearing immediately after the service grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The earthworks package CTA occupies the final content band of the page, directly beneath the pagination controls, acting as a visual anchor at the bottom of the main column. It spans the full width of the centered content container and uses slightly more vertical padding than surrounding sections (around 32–40px top and bottom) to make it feel important yet still in line with the conservative style. The CTA content is aligned left: a short, bold heading sits above or to the left of a concise line of explanatory text, with a prominent button placed immediately to the right of the text or beneath it on smaller widths. The button itself does not stretch full width; instead, it is sized to the label with around 12px vertical and 24px horizontal padding, echoing navigation item padding. There is approximately 24–32px of vertical space above the CTA separating it cleanly from the pagination, and a comfortable buffer of 40px or so below it before the page footer starts, clearly signaling the end of the page’s main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block section; remains at the bottom of the scrollable content. On narrower screens, text and button stack vertically while staying left‑aligned within the same band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services loading indicator appears in the same vertical region allocated to the earthworks service list. When active, it replaces or overlays the grid area directly under the filter bar. It is centered both horizontally and vertically within a fixed minimum height area (approximately the height of one to two rows of cards, e.g., 240–320px), ensuring it feels like a substantial band rather than a small spinner floating in whitespace. A simple spinner or progress icon sits above a short status line such as “Loading services…”, both centrally aligned. The surrounding margins above (distance to the filter bar) and below (distance to pagination) remain consistent with the normal service list spacing, preserving the banded visual rhythm even while data is loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the service list band while active, either replacing the grid or overlaying it with a semi‑transparent background. It scrolls with the content and does not affect the placement of sections above or below beyond temporarily changing the grid’s contents.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filters loading indicator is contained within the project‑type filter bar band. When filter options are loading or updating, a small spinner and brief label (e.g., “Updating filters…”) appear aligned to the right side of the filter bar’s content area, opposite the primary controls on the left. It does not significantly change the bar’s height; it sits vertically centered within the existing 16–24px padding. The filter controls themselves may appear dimmed or disabled while the indicator is visible, but the overall bar maintains its consistent shape and position between the hero and the service grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the filter bar’s horizontal layout, anchored to the right edge. It appears and disappears without altering the filter bar’s overall placement or pushing other sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services empty state occupies the space normally used by the earthworks service list, directly beneath the filter bar. It is centered within the content container and uses a minimum height similar to one to two rows of cards so that it still feels like a substantial content band. A simple illustrative icon or muted graphic can appear at the top, followed by a concise heading such as “No services match your filters” and a short explanatory line, all center‑aligned. Optionally, a small link or button to clear filters can be placed beneath the text. The vertical margins between the empty state block and the filter bar above, as well as the pagination/CTA below, mirror those of the normal grid, preserving the stacked band structure. Pagination may be hidden when the empty state is active to avoid confusion.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Reuses the service grid’s spatial slot; appears only when there are no results. It expands or contracts based on message length but maintains a minimum band height to keep the layout visually balanced.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data error banner forms a shallow but visually strong band at the very top of the main content column, directly beneath the global navigation and above the hero section. It spans the full width of the centered content container and uses a contrasting background color (e.g., red or deep amber) with white or dark text, making it immediately noticeable. Internally, it has about 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, with the error icon and message left‑aligned and any close/dismiss action aligned to the right. There is a modest 16–24px margin beneath the banner before the hero title begins, so it reads as its own distinct notification band without crowding the page heading. When not present, the hero section shifts up naturally to sit just below the navigation bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element injected at the top of the content stack; it pushes the hero and all subsequent sections downward while visible. It scrolls with the page and may be dismissible, after which it is removed entirely without leaving additional space.

name: Earthworks Package Configurator
title: Earthworks Package Configurator
description: Configure a custom earthworks machine-hours package, preview total hours and estimated cost against limits, and submit the configuration as a quote request.

core_features: Allow users to create and manage a list of earthworks package items (each with item_type, label, machine_hours, optional rate_per_hour and notes).
Allow users to specify package-level constraints such as a maximum total machine_hours limit and a maximum budget limit.

Use the previewEarthworksPackageConfiguration interface to calculate and display total_machine_hours, estimated_total_cost, currency, and whether the configuration is within the specified hours_limit and budget_limit.
Visually indicate when the current package is outside the hours_limit or budget_limit based on the latest preview response.
Provide a quote request form that captures project_name, project_description, site_postcode, project_type, contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, and optional budget_cap.
On quote submission, use the submitEarthworksPackageQuoteRequest interface to create an EarthworksPackageConfiguration and a linked QuoteRequest based on the configured items and user details.
Display a confirmation state using the response from submitEarthworksPackageQuoteRequest, including the package_configuration summary and quote_request_id when available.
Provide a navigational link to contact.html with the required URL parameters for users who need assistance configuring their earthworks package.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Load configurator with optional project context: 1) User navigates from earthworks_services.html via a link that may include a project_type URL parameter. 2) On page load, the configurator reads the project_type query parameter. 3) If the parameter matches a supported QuoteRequest.project_type option, the project_type field in the quote request form is prefilled. 4) The page initializes with an empty items list and no preview results until the user starts configuring the package.

Workflow 2 – Configure package items and constraints: 1) User sets or reviews package constraints (hours_limit and budget_limit) in a dedicated form. 2) User adds one or more earthworks items via an editable list/table, choosing an item_type from the available enum values, entering a label, machine_hours, and optionally rate_per_hour and notes. 3) Each creation, deletion, or change of an item or constraint triggers an event to the controller. 4) The controller aggregates the current items and constraint values and calls previewEarthworksPackageConfiguration. 5) The response updates the package preview summary, showing total_machine_hours, estimated_total_cost, and limit validation flags.

Workflow 3 – Review package preview and adjust configuration: 1) User observes total_machine_hours and estimated_total_cost in the summary area, including indicators showing whether the package is within hours_limit and budget_limit. 2) If the package exceeds any limit, the user adjusts machine_hours, removes items, or updates limits as appropriate. 3) Each adjustment triggers another previewEarthworksPackageConfiguration call until the user is satisfied with the configuration.

Workflow 4 – Fill in quote request details and submit: 1) Once the user is satisfied with the configuration and preview, they scroll to (or focus on) the quote request form. 2) The form may already have project_type from URL state; user completes project_name, project_description, site_postcode, contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, and optional budget_cap. 3) User submits the form. 4) The controller validates that required fields are present and that at least one item exists, then calls submitEarthworksPackageQuoteRequest with configuration_name (if set), items, hours_limit, budget_limit, and all project/contact fields. 5) On success, the controller stores and exposes the returned package_configuration, quote_request_id, status, success flag, and message to a confirmation component. 6) The confirmation component displays a concise summary of the submitted package and informs the user that the quote request has been received.

Workflow 5 – Use help link for assistance: 1) At any time, the user can click a 'Need help with configuration?' link. 2) The page navigates to contact.html with enquiry_type and subject URL parameters set as specified in the navigation configuration. 3) The user can then continue their enquiry from the contact page.
interactions: On page load: The URL-state handler reads the project_type URL parameter and initializes in-memory state for project_type, hours_limit, budget_limit, configuration_name (if present), and an empty items list. It may perform an initial previewEarthworksPackageConfiguration call with current constraints and items if desired by the implementation.

Add item interaction: When the user clicks 'Add item', a new EarthworksPackageItem row is added to the items editor in memory. This triggers an event to the controller, which then calls previewEarthworksPackageConfiguration with the updated items and current hours_limit and budget_limit.

Edit item interaction: When the user changes any field on an item row (item_type, label, machine_hours, rate_per_hour, notes), the items editor updates its internal representation and notifies the controller. On blur or debounce (implementation-specific), the controller calls previewEarthworksPackageConfiguration with the updated items and constraints.

Remove item interaction: When the user removes an item, the items editor updates the items list and triggers the controller to call previewEarthworksPackageConfiguration with the updated list and existing limits.

Update constraints interaction: When the user adjusts the hours_limit or budget_limit in the constraints form, the form updates its local state and notifies the controller. The controller calls previewEarthworksPackageConfiguration with the current items and the new limits, updating the preview summary and validation indicators.

Manual preview interaction (optional): If the implementation includes a 'Recalculate' or 'Preview package' button, clicking it aggregates the current items and constraints and calls previewEarthworksPackageConfiguration. The preview summary component is then updated with the returned totals and limit flags.

Quote form input interaction: As the user types into project_name, project_description, site_postcode, project_type, contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, and budget_cap fields, the form updates its local QuoteRequest draft state. project_type defaults from URL state if available and valid but remains editable.

Quote form submission interaction: On form submission, the quote form component performs client-side validation and emits a submit event. The controller handles this event by packaging configuration_name, items, hours_limit, budget_limit, and all quote fields into a call to submitEarthworksPackageQuoteRequest. Based on the returned response, it updates confirmation state and may also push a quote_request_id parameter into the URL for direct reference.

Quote submission success interaction: When submitEarthworksPackageQuoteRequest returns with success=true, the confirmation component renders a summary using the returned package_configuration and quote_request_id. The configurator UI may switch to a read-only state or keep the configuration editable according to implementation rules, but the confirmation remains visible.

Quote submission failure interaction: When submitEarthworksPackageQuoteRequest returns with success=false or an error occurs, the controller passes the message to the quote form component to display an error state, while keeping the user’s input and configuration intact for correction and resubmission.

Help navigation interaction: When the user clicks the 'Need help with configuration?' link, the browser navigates to contact.html with enquiry_type and subject query parameters appended as defined. No interfaces are called from this interaction on the configurator page.

URL synchronization interaction (optional): When the user changes project_type, configuration_name, hours_limit, or budget_limit, the controller may update the browser URL query parameters to reflect the current configuration context, enabling the user to refresh or share the URL while preserving key high-level settings (but not necessarily the full items list).
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven primarily by URL query parameters and in-memory configuration data. On initial load, the page reads the project_type parameter from the URL. If its value matches one of the supported QuoteRequest.project_type enum options, that value is used to prefill the project_type field in the quote request form; otherwise, the field is left unset for the user to choose. The implementation may also support additional URL query parameters such as configuration_name, hours_limit, and budget_limit for initializing the configurator; if these parameters are present, they populate the respective fields before any API calls. The items list itself is managed in memory during the session; it is not persisted via localStorage and may optionally be encoded into the URL query string or omitted from the URL depending on technical constraints. When configuration-related fields that are mirrored in the URL (e.g., project_type, configuration_name, hours_limit, budget_limit) change, the controller can update the URL parameters using history APIs so that the current context is preserved on reload without storing data locally. The previewEarthworksPackageConfiguration interface is invoked whenever there are changes to items or constraints; its response becomes the authoritative source for total_machine_hours, estimated_total_cost, currency, hours_limit, budget_limit, and limit flags, which are then bound to the preview summary component. When submitEarthworksPackageQuoteRequest is called on form submission, the returned package_configuration and quote_request_id are stored in in-memory state and may optionally be reflected in the URL as a quote_request_id parameter to allow the user to bookmark or reference the submitted configuration. Throughout, the page does not rely on localStorage; all persistent or shareable state is managed via URL parameters and server-side storage implicitly handled by the submitEarthworksPackageQuoteRequest interface.

components.functionality: Reads query parameters such as project_type, configuration_name, hours_limit, and budget_limit from the URL on page load and initializes the in-memory configuration and quote form state accordingly.

Provides input fields for users to specify or adjust package-level constraints including hours_limit and budget_limit; keeps these values in sync with configuration state and triggers recalculation via the controller when they change.

Allows users to add, edit, and remove earthworks package items. Each row captures item_type (using the EarthworksPackageItem.item_type enum), label, machine_hours, optional rate_per_hour, and notes. Any change triggers a configuration update event for recalculation.

Coordinates all changes to items and constraints, aggregates the current configuration, and calls previewEarthworksPackageConfiguration whenever necessary. It stores the latest preview response and exposes it to the preview summary component and validation states in the UI.

Displays the latest preview results returned by previewEarthworksPackageConfiguration, including total_machine_hours, estimated_total_cost, currency, hours_limit, budget_limit, and whether the package is within each limit. Provides visual validation cues based on is_within_hours_limit and is_within_budget_limit.

Allows the user to optionally name the current earthworks package configuration. This name is included when submitEarthworksPackageQuoteRequest is called and may be reflected in the URL for easy reference.

Captures quote-related information including project_name, project_description, site_postcode, project_type, contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, and optional budget_cap. It uses any prefilled project_type from URL-state and validates required fields before emitting a submission event.

Handles quote form submission events. It compiles configuration_name, current items, hours_limit, budget_limit, and all quote form fields and calls submitEarthworksPackageQuoteRequest. It then processes the response, storing the returned package_configuration, quote_request_id, status, success flag, and message, and updates the UI via the confirmation component and any relevant validation messages.

Displays the outcome of submitEarthworksPackageQuoteRequest. On success, it shows a concise summary of the created package_configuration (including total_machine_hours, estimated_total_cost, hours_limit, budget_limit, and limit flags) along with quote_request_id and message. On failure, it shows the error message and guidance to retry or adjust inputs.
