Provides a link labeled for configuration assistance. When clicked, navigates to contact.html with enquiry_type and subject URL parameters set according to the outgoing navigation specification.

Keeps selected configuration fields (such as project_type, configuration_name, hours_limit, and budget_limit) in sync with the browser URL query parameters without using localStorage, enabling refresh and shareable links that preserve high-level configuration context.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop-oriented grid typical of classic WordPress themes, with the main content constrained to a centered max-width container and internal content (like the 4 images) snapping to equal fractional columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a classic stacked-band layout mirroring the mockup: site header and dark nav bar (from the global template), followed by a centered content container. Inside this container, the top band is a hero text block with the H1 'Earthworks Package Configurator', a short explanatory subheading, and a subtle horizontal rule. Directly beneath, a full-width interactive band houses the earthworks items editor, visually echoing the four-image strip by arranging items in a grid that reads left-to-right. Below that, the content splits into two unequal columns: a narrower left column for configuration inputs (constraints, configuration name) and a wider right column for live preview and quote request. URL/state components operate invisibly, tied to the page lifecycle and forms without occupying visual space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a classic stacked-band layout mirroring the mockup: site header and dark nav bar (from the global template), followed by a centered content container. Inside this container, the top band is a hero text block with the H1 'Earthworks Package Configurator', a short explanatory subheading, and a subtle horizontal rule. Directly beneath, a full-width interactive band houses the earthworks items editor, visually echoing the four-image strip by arranging items in a grid that reads left-to-right. Below that, the content splits into two unequal columns: a narrower left column for configuration inputs (constraints, configuration name) and a wider right column for live preview and quote request. URL/state components operate invisibly, tied to the page lifecycle and forms without occupying visual space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content is constrained to a centered container spanning roughly 80–90% of the viewport width with a max-width around 1100–1200px, similar to a classic 12-column WordPress layout. The hero band occupies a moderate vertical height with generous top and bottom padding (~24–32px). The earthworks items editor band is visually prominent and takes up a notable vertical chunk (~25–30% of the first viewport height on desktop), stretching the full content width with narrow internal gutters. The lower two-column section fills the remaining vertical space; its left column uses about 35–40% of the container width, while the right column uses about 60–65%, separated by a gutter of ~24px. Vertical gaps between major bands (hero → items editor → two-column section) stay around 24–40px, following the mockup’s clear banding.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density steps up gradually from sparse to moderately dense. The hero band is light and open with minimal elements and comfortable white space. The items editor band is visually busy but organized, similar to a four-thumbnail row with compact spacing to maximize horizontal usage. The two-column region is the densest area: the left column stacks form fields with clear grouping and headings, while the right column combines the preview, controls, and quote form in a slightly denser arrangement, akin to the bullet-heavy text columns in the mockup. Overall, content remains readable and structured, never cluttered.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye starts at the global header and dark nav (with a contextual Help link on the right), then drops to the left-aligned H1 and subheading. From the hero, the gaze moves downward to the full-width earthworks items editor, scanning left-to-right across machinery or line items, similar to the four-image sweep. After reviewing items, the eye flows down into the split section: first to the left column for package constraints and configuration naming (analogous to the 'SERVICE' column), then across to the right column where the live package preview, controls, and quote request form sit (analogous to 'FEATURES & APPLICATIONS'). Interaction-wise, the flow is configure items → refine constraints → check preview → submit quote, with URL/state controllers silently keeping configuration and URL in sync in the background.

layout.overall_description: The Earthworks Package Configurator page sits within a conservative, corporate-style template: an oval logo and contact details in a shallow header, a strong dark navigation bar beneath (including a subtle Help link), and then a centered content column below. Inside the content area, a left-aligned H1 and short subheading introduce the configurator, framed with comfortable white space and grounded by a thin horizontal rule. Immediately under this hero text, a wide interactive band presents the earthworks items editor in a grid that stretches nearly the full content width, visually echoing the mockup’s four-image machinery strip and encouraging a left-to-right scan of machines and hours. Beneath this strip, the layout breaks into two columns: on the left, a narrower settings panel stacks the package constraints form and configuration name input under a clear heading, forming a concise control area; on the right, a wider preview and quote panel houses the package preview controller at the top, the main preview summary card in the middle, and the quote request form and confirmation feedback below it. The entire page reads in clean horizontal bands separated by consistent 24–40px vertical gaps, with invisible controllers and URL handlers wired into forms and interactions but not consuming visual space, preserving the straightforward, content-first aesthetic of an older but solid business theme.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is functionally positioned at the very top of the page’s main content stack, logically just inside the centered content container but above the hero band in the DOM order. It does not render any visible UI, occupies no width or height, and introduces no extra spacing. From a layout perspective, it behaves like a hidden initialization layer that runs before the user sees the hero H1, ensuring that any configuration encoded in the URL is loaded before content-dependent components (such as the items editor and preview) appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, hidden system component; inline with the DOM at the top of the main content but occupying zero visual space and not affecting flow or spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package constraints form sits in the lower half of the page within the left column of the two-column band, directly beneath the earthworks items editor and aligned to the left edge of the centered content container. It starts roughly 24–32px below the bottom of the items editor band, matching the vertical gaps seen between sections in the mockup. The form is contained in a simple rectangular block with a light border or subtle background tint, spanning about 35–40% of the content width. Inside, fields are stacked vertically with 16–24px of internal padding and 12–16px gaps between field groups, creating a moderately dense but ordered feel similar to the 'SERVICE' bullet list column. A clear heading such as 'Package Constraints' appears at the top of the block, with the configuration name input stacked just below this form in the same column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; it scrolls with the page. On narrower screens, it falls above the preview column content, maintaining a linear top-to-bottom order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The earthworks items editor is the main interactive band directly below the hero text. It is centered within the main content container, beginning about 24–32px under the horizontal rule that closes the hero section. Horizontally, it spans nearly the full width of the content container (roughly 90–100% of the container), with 16–24px of internal padding on the left and right to keep items from touching the edges. Inside, the editor presents earthworks item rows in a grid that visually echoes the four-image strip: on desktop, the primary items are arranged into up to four equal-width columns across the width, each column occupying about 1/4 of the usable width with narrow 8–16px gutters. Rows or cards for each machine type show image/icon, name, hour inputs, and possibly rate, tightly but clearly laid out to give a slightly dense, utilitarian feel. Vertically, the band takes a medium height, roughly 25–30% of the initial viewport, with a defined top and bottom edge clearly separate from both the hero above and the two-column section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static primary content band within the scrolling page; on smaller screens, the four-column grid collapses to two or one columns while retaining its banded separation from hero and lower forms.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package preview controller is positioned at the top of the right-hand column in the lower two-column band, roughly parallel in vertical alignment to the top of the package constraints form on the left. It sits about 24–32px under the bottom of the earthworks items editor, sharing the same horizontal baseline as the left column’s heading. The controller appears as a compact horizontal strip of controls—such as toggles, view mode switches, or recalculation buttons—spanning the full width of the right column (about 60–65% of content width). It has modest vertical padding (8–12px) and minimal decoration, aligning visually with the navigation bar’s functional, no-frills style but in a lighter tone. Immediately below, separated by an 8–16px gap, lies the package preview summary card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, top-anchored within the right column; on tall viewports it may sit near the middle of the screen once scrolled into view but does not stick or float by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package preview summary sits directly beneath the package preview controller in the right-hand column, forming the visual core of that column. It occupies the full available width of the right column (around 60–65% of the content container), with 16–24px of internal padding and a clear bounding box—either a subtle border or slightly shaded background—to set it apart from the surrounding page, similar in visual weight to a prominent text panel or feature list in the mockup. Vertically, it is taller than any single form on the left, often extending down to encompass total hours, estimated cost, and any warnings about exceeding limits. The top of this panel starts about 8–16px below the controller, and its bottom edge is about 24px above the quote-request form, creating a balanced vertical rhythm while still making the preview feel like a continuous block with its related actions below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static panel within the right column; on wider screens it may be tall enough to encourage scrolling while the left column content ends slightly sooner, maintaining a right-heavy visual anchor as the user moves down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The configuration name input is positioned within the left column, directly beneath the package constraints form, aligning flush with the same left and right edges. It begins around 16–24px below the bottom of the constraints form, maintaining the same column width (about 35–40% of the content container). It is presented as a simple labeled text input, possibly inside a small sub-block with a label like 'Configuration Name', with modest padding (8–16px) and standard field height to keep the control visually understated. This placement keeps naming the configuration as a natural follow-up step to setting constraints, mirroring how the mockup stacks related text content within the narrower left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the left column; scrolls normally and maintains its stacked relationship under the constraints form across breakpoints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote request form is located in the right column directly beneath the package preview summary, starting about 24px below the bottom of the preview panel to create a clear separation between preview and action. It spans the full width of the right column (about 60–65% of the content container) and extends downward with a series of stacked fields for contact details and any additional notes. The form uses moderate vertical spacing (12–16px between fields, 16–24px internal padding), giving it a similar information density to the bullet-heavy text lists in the mockup’s lower section. A straightforward heading such as 'Request a Quote' aligns with the left edge of the column, and the primary submit button is aligned to the right or centered at the bottom, with ~16–24px of space below it before the start of the confirmation panel area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable section of the right column. On narrow screens, it stacks below the preview summary and above the confirmation panel in a single-column flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote submission controller is conceptually attached to the quote request form in the right column. Visually, it does not render any controls or occupy additional space; instead, it functions as a behind-the-scenes handler for form submission and status. In the layout, it sits immediately adjacent to or wrapped around the quote-request form in the DOM, but from the user’s perspective there is no extra block or gap introduced—the layout appears as a continuous form panel leading directly into the confirmation feedback area below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-height behavioral layer tied to the quote-request form; it does not affect spacing or the flow of surrounding components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote confirmation panel resides in the right column, logically positioned directly beneath the quote request form. By default, it either remains hidden or appears as a compact area that uses the same horizontal width as the form (60–65% of the content container). When active (after submission), it presents a short confirmation message and possibly a summary reference, using a lightly highlighted background or border to distinguish it from the form above, similar to a subtle notice bar or a small text block at the bottom of a column in the mockup. It begins about 16–24px below the form’s submit button area, with 12–16px of internal padding, and ends with a modest bottom margin (~24–32px) before the end of the main content area, tying off the right column’s content in a tidy way.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; appears or hides in place without shifting the relative structure of surrounding components beyond normal content expansion.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help navigation link is integrated into the dark global navigation bar that sits directly beneath the site header. It is positioned on the far right of the nav bar, aligned horizontally with the other menu items, but styled slightly more subtly (smaller font or lighter color) so it feels like a utility link rather than a primary navigation item. The link uses the same nav padding rhythm—about 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal—ensuring it sits comfortably within the bar’s height. From a flow perspective, it is encountered immediately after the main nav items as the eye scans left-to-right across the dark band, providing quick access to configurator help without disrupting the content-focused bands below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed within the global navigation bar area at the top of the page; it remains visible as long as the navigation bar is visible and scrolls out of view with the header if the header is not sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL sync controller is logically associated with the main interactive configurator components (items editor, constraints form, preview, and quote form) but does not render any visible UI. Layout-wise, it sits inline within the main content container, typically near or wrapped around the central configurator section, yet it occupies no physical space and creates no additional margins or padding. Its presence is only reflected behaviorally as changes to items, constraints, or preview state are mirrored in the URL, preserving the classic, uncluttered content-first layout where only functional components are visually represented.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-size background controller that does not affect spatial layout or scrolling; it simply listens to and updates state in sync with user interactions.

name: Pile Estimator
title: Pile Estimator
description: Interactive calculator to estimate pile quantities and costs based on building parameters, and to submit a detailed quote request using the calculated estimate.

core_features: Input form for building parameters (length, width, soil type, building use type, optional project type, optional budget threshold) and calculation of a pile estimate via the calculatePileEstimate interface.

Display of the calculated estimate, including estimated pile quantity, total cost, currency, and an indication of whether the estimate is under the provided budget threshold using the is_under_budget_threshold flag from calculatePileEstimate.

Ability to request a detailed quote based on a specific pile estimate via the submitPileEstimatorQuoteRequest interface, capturing project name, site postcode, project type, contact details, and optional budget cap.

Flow control and state management using URL parameters so that project type can be prefilled from referring pages, estimates can be reloaded or shared, and quote submission states can be reflected without using localStorage.
Navigation to the general contact page with predefined enquiry_type and subject URL parameters after a quote request, to allow further discussion about the estimate.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Open estimator from another page with preselected project type
1. User clicks a Pile Estimator link on index.html or services.html, which navigates to pile_estimator.html with a project_type URL parameter.
2. On page load, the state manager reads project_type from the URL.
3. The estimator input form preselects the project_type field accordingly.
4. User completes required fields (building_length_m, building_width_m, soil_type, building_use_type) and optionally adjusts project_type and budget_threshold.
5. User submits the estimator form, triggering a call to calculatePileEstimate with the current input values.
6. On success, the page updates the results component, stores estimate_id in the URL, and reflects the calculated values and whether the estimate is under the budget threshold.

Workflow 2: Share or reload a previously configured estimate
1. User has a URL to pile_estimator.html that includes estimator input parameters and an estimate_id (e.g., building_length_m, building_width_m, soil_type, building_use_type, project_type, budget_threshold, estimate_id in the query string).
2. On page load, the state manager reads the URL parameters.
3. If all required calculation parameters are present, the state manager automatically invokes calculatePileEstimate with those parameters.
4. When the response is returned, the estimator input form is populated with the parameters from the URL, and the results component displays the current estimate data.
5. User can modify any inputs and resubmit the form to refresh the estimate, which will also update the URL parameters and estimate_id.

Workflow 3: Request a detailed quote from the estimate
1. After a successful estimate, the results component shows a 'Request Detailed Quote' action.
2. User clicks this action; the state manager updates the URL (e.g., with a mode indicating quote step and the current estimate_id) and reveals or focuses the quote request form.
3. The quote request form is prefilled with project_type from the estimate (or from the URL project_type if available), and includes fields for project_name, site_postcode, contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, and optional budget_cap.
4. User submits the quote form, which calls submitPileEstimatorQuoteRequest with estimate_id and the entered project details.
5. On success, the page displays a confirmation message and updates the URL with quote_request_id and a quote_status indicator.
6. A 'Contact us about this estimate' link is shown, which navigates to contact.html with enquiry_type and subject URL parameters and may also include the quote_request_id and estimate_id as additional parameters if desired.

Workflow 4: Follow up via contact page after quote submission
1. After a successful quote request, user clicks 'Contact us about this estimate'.
2. The browser navigates to contact.html with URL parameters including enquiry_type set to the configured quote enquiry value and subject set to the configured pile estimator follow-up subject (as per the outgoing connection specification).
3. The contact page can then use these parameters to preselect the enquiry type and subject for the user.
interactions: User updates a numeric building_length_m input field: the estimator form component updates its internal state for building_length_m and keeps it in sync with the URL only when the form is submitted.
User updates a numeric building_width_m input field: the estimator form component stores the value locally until submission.
User selects a soil_type from an options list tied to the PileEstimate.soil_type enum: the estimator form updates its soil_type state.
User selects a building_use_type from options tied to the PileEstimate.building_use_type enum: the estimator form updates its building_use_type state.

User selects or confirms a project_type (if present in the URL): the estimator form updates the project_type field, using enum options consistent with PileEstimate.project_type and QuoteRequest.project_type.

User optionally enters a budget_threshold numeric value: the estimator form stores this value and passes it to calculatePileEstimate, allowing the backend to determine whether the estimate is under budget.

User clicks the estimator form submit button: onSubmit handler validates required fields and then invokes calculatePileEstimate with building_length_m, building_width_m, soil_type, building_use_type, project_type, and budget_threshold.

On successful calculatePileEstimate response: the state manager updates the URL query parameters (including estimate_id and the calculation parameters), the results component binds to the returned data, and an indicator for is_under_budget_threshold is displayed.
On calculatePileEstimate error or invalid input: the estimator form shows validation or error messages without changing URL parameters related to estimate_id.

User clicks 'Request Detailed Quote' in the results component: an onClick handler updates the URL (e.g., adding a quote_mode flag and the current estimate_id), then ensures the quote request form is visible and prefilled where possible.

User inputs project_name, site_postcode, contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, and optional budget_cap in the quote request form: the form stores these values in its local state until submission.

User submits the quote request form: onSubmit handler validates required fields and invokes submitPileEstimatorQuoteRequest using estimate_id from the latest estimate and the entered project/contact details.

On successful submitPileEstimatorQuoteRequest response: the state manager updates the URL with quote_request_id and a quote_status flag, and the quote status/confirmation component displays a success message.
On failed submitPileEstimatorQuoteRequest response: the quote form displays error messaging and allows retry without losing previously entered data.

User clicks the 'Contact us about this estimate' link displayed after a successful quote submission: the browser navigates to contact.html with enquiry_type and subject URL parameters set according to the outgoing connection configuration.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page does not use localStorage; all persistent/resharable state is maintained via URL query parameters and in-memory component state.

URL parameters:
- project_type: Optional, accepted on initial load. If present, it is used to prefill the project_type field in the estimator form and to set an initial project_type for any subsequent quote request. If the user changes project_type and submits a new estimate, the new value is written back to the URL.
- building_length_m, building_width_m, soil_type, building_use_type, budget_threshold: When an estimate is successfully calculated, these parameters are written to the URL to represent the inputs that produced the current estimate. On page load, if these parameters are present and valid, the state manager uses them to prefill the estimator form and may auto-invoke calculatePileEstimate to reconstruct the estimate state.
- estimate_id: On a successful calculatePileEstimate response, the returned estimate_id is added or updated in the URL. The quote request form uses this estimate_id when calling submitPileEstimatorQuoteRequest. If the page is loaded with an estimate_id and compatible input parameters, the page treats it as the active estimate and will request a fresh calculation using those parameters.
- quote_mode (or a similar flag): When the user clicks 'Request Detailed Quote', the page updates the URL with a mode indicator (e.g., quote_mode=true). On page load, if this flag is present and estimate_id is also present, the quote form is automatically shown and prefilled.
- quote_request_id: When submitPileEstimatorQuoteRequest returns successfully, the generated quote_request_id is added to the URL. This allows the user to reload or share the page and still see that a quote request has been submitted for the given estimate.
- quote_status: An additional URL parameter that indicates the status of the last quote submission from this page (e.g., success or error). On load, if quote_status indicates success and quote_request_id is present, the confirmation component displays a success state; if it indicates error, the quote form shows an error state.

State transitions:
- Initial load: The state manager parses URL parameters. If only project_type is present, it just prepopulates the form. If all calculation parameters are present, it triggers calculatePileEstimate. If quote_mode and estimate_id are also present, it shows both the estimator results and the quote form.
- After calculation: The page writes calculation parameters and estimate_id into the URL, resetting any quote_status or quote_request_id values from previous sessions to avoid confusion.
- After quote submission: The page appends quote_request_id and quote_status to the URL. Any subsequent navigation back to this URL can reestablish the 'quote submitted' view. The 'Contact us about this estimate' link uses fixed enquiry_type and subject values specified by the outgoing connection configuration when navigating to contact.html.

components.functionality: Coordinates page-level state by reading and writing URL parameters, orchestrating calls to calculatePileEstimate on page load or form submission, and controlling whether the quote request form and confirmation views are visible.

Collects building parameters (building_length_m, building_width_m, soil_type, building_use_type), optional project_type and budget_threshold, validates them, and triggers the calculatePileEstimate interface to compute a new pile estimate.

Displays the current estimate data returned from calculatePileEstimate, including estimated_pile_quantity, estimated_total_cost, currency, and whether the estimate is under the budget threshold using is_under_budget_threshold. Provides a 'Request Detailed Quote' action when a valid estimate is available.

Captures project and contact details required for a detailed quote request based on the currently active pile estimate, including estimate_id, project_name, site_postcode, project_type, contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, and optional budget_cap, then calls submitPileEstimatorQuoteRequest.

Shows the outcome of submitPileEstimatorQuoteRequest, including success or error messages based on the returned success and status fields, and exposes a 'Contact us about this estimate' navigation link when the quote request is successful.

Reads relevant query parameters (project_type, building_length_m, building_width_m, soil_type, building_use_type, budget_threshold, estimate_id, quote_mode, quote_request_id, quote_status) on load and updates them when estimates or quotes are created, ensuring the current page state is reflected in the URL for bookmarking and sharing.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop-oriented grid typical of classic WordPress themes, with the main content constrained to a centered max-width container and internal content (like the 4 images) snapping to equal fractional columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the conservative, banded, top-to-bottom structure from the mockup: a compact logo/contact header at the very top, followed by a full-width dark navigation bar, then a centered main content container. Inside this container, the flow is: page title and short intro (hero text), a subtle horizontal rule, then a prominent calculator band where the pile estimator form and results sit side by side. Below that, a secondary band mirrors the two-column SERVICE / FEATURES layout from the mockup, with a narrower left column for quote status and a wider right column for the quote request form. State and URL sync components are non-visual and conceptually wrap or sit behind this main content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the conservative, banded, top-to-bottom structure from the mockup: a compact logo/contact header at the very top, followed by a full-width dark navigation bar, then a centered main content container. Inside this container, the flow is: page title and short intro (hero text), a subtle horizontal rule, then a prominent calculator band where the pile estimator form and results sit side by side. Below that, a secondary band mirrors the two-column SERVICE / FEATURES layout from the mockup, with a narrower left column for quote status and a wider right column for the quote request form. State and URL sync components are non-visual and conceptually wrap or sit behind this main content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and navigation occupy shallow vertical bands, leaving most of the vertical space for the estimator and quote content. The main content container spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, centered, using an implicit 12-column grid. The calculator band is given generous vertical height and internal padding, echoing the importance of the image strip in the mockup. Within that band, the form occupies about 7 of 12 columns on the left, and the results panel 5 of 12 on the right. The quote band below uses a narrower left column (about 4 of 12 columns) for status and a wider right column (about 8 of 12) for the quote form. Vertical gaps of roughly 24–40px separate major bands, with 16–24px padding inside each content box, following the mockup’s spacing rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero area remains open and light, with ample whitespace around the H1 and subheading. The calculator band has moderate density: a reasonably detailed form on the left balanced by a structured but not cramped results panel on the right. The lower quote band leans slightly denser, similar to the mockup’s SERVICE and FEATURES lists: more fields and status details arranged in multi-line content, but controlled with consistent line spacing and clear grouping. Overall density increases as the user scrolls down, mirroring the mockup’s transition from airy hero to denser text columns.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the logo on the top left to the contact details on the top right, then down to the dark navigation bar with horizontal menu items. From there, attention drops to the left-aligned H1 ('Pile Estimator') and its subheading. A horizontal rule anchors the start of the functional area. The gaze then moves into the calculator band: first to the left-hand form where inputs begin, then horizontally across to the right-hand results panel to see immediate feedback. Scrolling further down, the eye encounters the quote band, starting with the left-hand quote status (acting as a contextual summary) and then moving to the right-hand quote request form, which is read top-to-bottom through grouped fields. Non-visual components (state manager and URL sync) support this flow without affecting the visual layout.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a classic, corporate WordPress-style layout with stacked horizontal bands. At the top, a slim header strip runs full width: the oval company logo sits anchored on the far left, while contact details (phone, email, locations) are stacked and right-aligned on the far right, all within a shallow 10–15% viewport-height band. Immediately below, a full-width dark navigation bar spans edge to edge, with menu items laid out horizontally and modest padding, giving a strong visual anchor. Under the nav, the main content is constrained to a centered, max-width container (roughly 70–80% of viewport width), sitting on a light background. The first thing inside is a left-aligned H1 'Pile Estimator' and a brief explanatory subheading, with generous top and bottom spacing. A subtle horizontal rule runs nearly the full width of the container, signaling the start of the interactive content. Directly beneath, the primary calculator band fills the width of the container: on the left two-thirds, a structured estimator form stacks labeled fields vertically; on the right one-third, a bordered results panel shows quantities, cost breakdowns, and summaries. This band feels like the page’s functional core, mirroring the visual prominence of the image strip in the original mockup. Below it, separated by a 24–40px vertical gap, a secondary band divides into two uneven columns: a narrower left column summarizing quote status and submission state, and a wider right column containing the full quote request form. Both columns align to the same baseline and share consistent padding, resembling the SERVICE and FEATURES columns from the mockup. Overall, the layout is linear and predictable, with clear left-to-right reading patterns inside each band and a steady vertical progression from overview (hero) to interaction (calculator) to commitment (quote request).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state-controller component does not render visible UI on the page. Conceptually, it wraps the entire main content area inside the centered content container that sits below the navigation bar. It occupies no dedicated space in the visual layout, but you can think of it as logically attached to the top of the estimator section, orchestrating the behavior of the calculator and quote components as the user moves through the vertically stacked bands.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; participates in the layout only as an invisible wrapper/controller with no rendered dimensions or position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pile estimator form sits in the main content container as the left-hand side of the primary calculator band, directly below the hero text and horizontal rule. It is positioned flush with the left edge of the centered container and spans roughly 60–65% of the container width (about 7–8 of 12 grid columns), leaving room for the results panel on its right. There is a vertical margin of about 24–32px above it, separating it from the hero area, and about 24–32px below to keep it distinct from the quote band that follows. The form’s content is arranged in a vertical stack: a small section heading at the top, followed by labeled input fields with 16–24px vertical gaps between field groups, matching the moderate density seen in the mockup’s text areas. Internally, the form has 16–24px padding on all sides, with a simple flat background (white or very light) and, optionally, a subtle 1px border or faint box-shadow to define it as a block, while remaining visually conservative. The right edge of the form aligns to an internal vertical grid line where the results panel begins, preserving the sense of a two-column layout within this band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level component within the main content flow; on narrower viewports it can stack above the results panel, but on desktop it remains a left-hand column in the calculator band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pile estimate results panel occupies the right-hand side of the same calculator band as the pile estimator form. It is aligned with the top of the form, so their top edges sit on the same horizontal line. Horizontally, it is anchored to the right edge of the centered content container and spans roughly 35–40% of the container width (about 4–5 of 12 grid columns). A vertical gap of around 16–24px separates it from the form to its left, mimicking the narrow column gaps in the mockup’s four-image row. The panel is slightly more compact than the form, with a clear title at the top (e.g., 'Estimate Summary'), followed by a vertical stack of result rows or grouped sections: quantities, estimated costs, and possibly a small list of assumptions. Internal padding is around 16–24px, with consistent line spacing to maintain a moderate but not cramped density. Visually, it may share the same flat background and subtle border treatment as the form, maintaining a unified banded look. Its bottom edge typically aligns near the bottom of the form, though it can extend slightly higher or lower depending on content; extra spacing below the shorter of the two ensures that the overall calculator band still reads as one coherent horizontal block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level component that sits beside the form within the same horizontal band; on smaller screens it can wrap to sit below the form while preserving vertical spacing, but on desktop it remains a right-hand column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pile estimator quote form sits in the secondary content band below the calculator band, occupying the wider right-hand column, analogous to the 'FEATURES & APPLICATIONS' section in the mockup. This band begins roughly 24–40px beneath the bottom of the calculator band, with a subtle increase in textual density. The quote form is aligned with the right edge of the centered content container and spans about 8 of 12 grid columns (two-thirds of the width), leaving the left third for the quote status panel. It starts at the same vertical line as the quote status on the left, so their headings align horizontally. Inside, the form fields are stacked vertically with labels, grouped into logical sections (e.g., Contact Details, Project Details, Additional Notes), each section separated by 16–24px of vertical space. Internal padding of 16–24px on all sides mirrors the estimator form above, and the overall block sits on a flat, light background with either no decoration or a very subtle border. The bottom of the form defines the lower edge of this band; the left status column generally terminates around the same vertical area or slightly above. Overall, this column carries a similar but slightly higher information density than the calculator band, consistent with the mockup’s lower text columns.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level component within the two-column quote band; remains on the right at desktop widths and can stack below the status panel on smaller screens while maintaining clear vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote status panel occupies the narrower left-hand column of the quote band beneath the calculator. It is positioned directly below the calculator band, with a vertical gap of about 24–40px separating the two bands, and is aligned with the left edge of the centered content container. It spans roughly 4 of 12 grid columns (about one-third of the width), with the quote form filling the remaining space to its right. The top of this panel aligns horizontally with the top of the quote form, creating a clean two-column start similar to the 'SERVICE' vs. 'FEATURES & APPLICATIONS' layout in the mockup. Inside, content density is moderate: a short heading (e.g., 'Quote Status'), followed by concise text or bullet points indicating whether an estimate has been generated, submission status, reference numbers, or last updated timestamps. Line spacing is comfortable but slightly tighter than the hero area, reflecting the more information-rich lower section of the page. Internal padding is 16–24px, and visual style matches the adjacent quote form (flat background, subtle border), so the two columns feel like parts of a single band. The height of this panel is typically less than or equal to that of the quote form; any additional vertical space is allowed to appear beneath it, keeping the band visually open at the bottom rather than forcing artificial equal heights.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level component; on narrow viewports it can stack above the quote form, acting as a contextual preface to the form, but on desktop it remains a left-hand column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This URL-sync controller is non-visual and does not occupy any explicit visual space. Conceptually, it is attached to the main content area encompassing both the calculator and quote bands, listening for changes in form inputs and results to sync with the URL. It sits behind the interface in the component tree, with no box, background, or visible affordance, and therefore does not affect spacing, alignment, or the banded structure of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; exists purely as behavioral infrastructure with no rendered layout footprint.

name: Equipment Hire Listing
title: Equipment Hire
description: Functional listing page where users can filter, sort, and browse hireable equipment (e.g., piling rigs, excavators, trucks, rollers), then navigate to equipment details or the booking review page.

core_features: Initialize available filter and sort options by calling getEquipmentFilterOptions and render them as interactive controls.
Read URL parameters (category, min_rating, max_daily_rate, sort_by, page) to determine the initial state of filters, sorting, and pagination.
Call listEquipment with the current filter, availability, sorting, and pagination parameters to retrieve and display a paginated list of equipment.
Allow users to change category, rating threshold, maximum daily rate, sort order, and availability, updating the URL parameters and reloading listEquipment accordingly.
Show total result count and current page, and provide pagination controls that update the page URL parameter and refresh the results via listEquipment.
Enable navigation to equipment_detail.html with the selected equipment id when a user clicks an equipment card title or a dedicated “View Details” action.
Provide a persistent toolbar link (button or icon) that navigates to booking.html so users can review their hire booking.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Landing with preselected category: (1) User clicks an Equipment Hire link from index.html or services.html that includes a category URL parameter. (2) Equipment Hire page reads the category (and any other query parameters) from the URL. (3) Page calls getEquipmentFilterOptions to build the filter controls. (4) Page sets the category filter UI to match the URL parameter. (5) Page calls listEquipment with category plus any other derived filter, availability, sort, and pagination parameters. (6) User sees the filtered list and may further refine filters or navigate to details.

Workflow 2 – Adjusting filters on the listing page: (1) User changes one or more filters (category, min_rating, max_daily_rate, availability toggle) in the filter controls. (2) Page logic updates the corresponding URL parameters (e.g., min_rating, max_daily_rate) and resets page to the first page in the URL. (3) Page calls listEquipment with the updated parameters. (4) Results grid updates to reflect the new filtered subset, and the results summary is updated.

Workflow 3 – Sorting results: (1) User selects a sort option (e.g., by price or rating) from the sort control populated from getEquipmentFilterOptions.sort_options. (2) Page updates the sort_by URL parameter and resets the page parameter to the first page. (3) Page calls listEquipment with the updated sort_by value and existing filter parameters. (4) Results grid re-renders in the new order.

Workflow 4 – Paginating through equipment: (1) User clicks a pagination control (e.g., next, previous, or a specific page number). (2) Page updates the page URL parameter to the chosen index. (3) Page calls listEquipment with the updated page value (and current filters/sort). (4) Results grid shows equipment for that page, and the pagination controls highlight the current page.

Workflow 5 – Viewing equipment details: (1) User clicks an equipment card title or a dedicated 'View Details' button within a card. (2) Page reads that equipment item’s id from the bound data returned by listEquipment. (3) Browser navigates to equipment_detail.html with id set as a URL parameter. (4) New page uses the id to load detailed information (handled outside this page).

Workflow 6 – Reviewing booking: (1) User clicks a 'Review booking' action or cart icon in the listing toolbar. (2) Browser navigates directly to booking.html (with or without additional query parameters as determined by global booking behavior, not by this page).
interactions: On page load, the controller component reads URL parameters, invokes getEquipmentFilterOptions, then invokes listEquipment with parameters derived from the URL and default values for any missing parameters.

When the user changes the category filter control, the page updates the category URL parameter (or removes it if the user chooses the unfiltered state), resets page to the first page, updates the URL, and re-calls listEquipment.
When the user adjusts the minimum rating filter, the page updates the min_rating URL parameter (or removes it if cleared), resets page to the first page, updates the URL, and re-calls listEquipment.

When the user adjusts the maximum daily rate filter, the page updates the max_daily_rate URL parameter (or removes it if cleared), resets page to the first page, updates the URL, and re-calls listEquipment.

When the user toggles an availability-only filter (e.g., 'Show available only'), the page updates an internal availability flag and may map it to the only_available parameter when calling listEquipment; if represented in the URL, the page updates the corresponding URL parameter, resets the page index, and re-calls listEquipment.
When the user changes the sort option, the page updates the sort_by URL parameter, resets page to the first page, updates the URL, and re-calls listEquipment.
When the user uses pagination controls, the page updates the page URL parameter, updates the URL, and re-calls listEquipment with the new page value.
When the user clicks an equipment card title or 'View Details' action, the page navigates to equipment_detail.html with id set to the equipment object's id from listEquipment.
When the user clicks the 'Review booking' or cart icon in the toolbar, the page navigates to booking.html without calling any additional interfaces from this page.

When the browser back/forward buttons are used, the controller listens for URL changes, re-reads the current parameters, updates UI controls to match them, and re-calls listEquipment so the view is consistent with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL query parameters as the primary source of truth for filter, sorting, and pagination state. On initialization and on any URL change, the controller parses the URL parameters category, min_rating, max_daily_rate, sort_by, and page. For each, if present and valid, the value is applied to the internal state and the corresponding UI control. If absent or invalid, a default behavior is applied (for example, no category filter, no rating filter, no price cap, default sort option defined in application configuration, and the first page; defaults are not hardcoded in this specification). The controller then calls listEquipment with a parameter set derived from this state: category (if any), min_rating (if any), max_daily_rate (if any), an availability flag mapped to only_available in the listEquipment call, sort_by (either from the URL or a default), page (from the URL or a default), and page_size obtained from application configuration or, if exposed, from a dedicated URL parameter. Any change in a filter, sort control, or pagination control triggers a URL update (using pushState or replaceState) and a new listEquipment call using the updated parameters. The getEquipmentFilterOptions interface is called at least once per page load (and on full reloads) to populate dynamic filter and sort options; its outputs determine available categories, rating thresholds, price ranges, and sort options. The page does not use localStorage or similar client-side persistence; state is derived solely from the URL and current API responses.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL parameter parsing, calls to getEquipmentFilterOptions and listEquipment, manages in-memory page state (current filters, sort, availability flag, and pagination), and passes data and callbacks to child components.

Displays filter and sort controls for equipment listing, including category selection, rating threshold, maximum daily rate, availability toggle, and sort order. It receives available options from the controller (sourced from getEquipmentFilterOptions) and emits events whenever a control value changes.
Displays the total number of matching equipment items and a concise summary of active filters (as derived from URL parameters and controller state) to provide context for the listing results.

Renders a paginated grid or list of equipment items returned by listEquipment. Each item shows key data (name, category, description snippet, daily_rate with currency, average_rating, rating_count, availability, and image) and provides a clickable title and dedicated 'View Details' action that navigates to equipment_detail.html with the corresponding id.

Displays pagination elements (e.g., next/previous controls and page index selectors) based on total_results from listEquipment and the current page and page_size values provided by the controller. Emits page change events that cause URL updates and new listEquipment calls.
Provides a persistent 'Review booking' or cart-style action that navigates the user to booking.html. It does not invoke any interfaces itself; it simply initiates navigation when activated.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop-oriented grid typical of classic WordPress themes, with the main content constrained to a centered max-width container and internal content (like the 4 images) snapping to equal fractional columns.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same stacked, top‑anchored horizontal bands as the mockup: within the main content area, start with a left-aligned page title/subheading band, followed by a strong functional band for filter/sort (analogous to the dark nav bar), then a lighter summary band, then a dense visual/content band for the equipment grid (echoing the four-image strip), and finally lower-importance utility bands for booking review and pagination. All of this sits inside a centered max-width container on a 12-column grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same stacked, top‑anchored horizontal bands as the mockup: within the main content area, start with a left-aligned page title/subheading band, followed by a strong functional band for filter/sort (analogous to the dark nav bar), then a lighter summary band, then a dense visual/content band for the equipment grid (echoing the four-image strip), and finally lower-importance utility bands for booking review and pagination. All of this sits inside a centered max-width container on a 12-column grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve a shallow vertical slice for the title/subheading (about 10–15% of the main content height), a slim but visually strong band for the filter toolbar, a compact band for the results summary, and then allow the equipment grid to dominate both horizontal and vertical space (roughly 50–60% of the scrollable content on a typical results page). The booking review toolbar and pagination controls sit below the grid as slimmer bands with moderate vertical padding. Horizontally, all components live inside the same max-width container (around 80–90% of viewport width, max-width ~1100–1200px), following a 12-column grid, with the grid items snapping to equal fractional columns.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep the top section (title and subheading) relatively open with generous vertical spacing and simple text. Increase density progressively as you move down: the filter/sort toolbar is compact but legible, the results summary is slightly denser, and the equipment grid is the densest area, with multiple cards per row and stacked details. The booking toolbar and pagination return to moderate density, using clear grouping and padding so they remain readable and uncluttered.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye in a clear vertical sequence, anchored by horizontal bands: first to the left-aligned page title and subheading, then down to the strong filter/sort band, then to the concise results summary. From there, the eye drops into the equipment grid, scanning rows of cards left to right. After reaching the bottom of the grid, the user naturally encounters the booking review toolbar (summary + primary action), and finally the pagination controls. Within each band, align primary content to the left and secondary controls to the right, mirroring the mockup’s logo/contact and navigation patterns.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s existing header and dark navigation bar, the Equipment Hire Listing page opens into a centered content column with a conservative business look: a left-aligned H1 and a short descriptive line breathe in a lightly padded band, capped by a thin horizontal rule. A darker, slim filter-and-sort bar sits immediately below, stretching the full width of the content container like a secondary navigation, with filters arranged from left to right. Under that, a compact summary line quietly reports how many pieces of equipment match the current criteria. The main body is dominated by a four-column grid of equipment cards, each a rectangular tile with a machinery image on top and key details below, filling the width with narrow gaps to maximize visible options. At the bottom of the grid, a slightly emphasized booking review toolbar forms another horizontal band: on the left, a simple text summary of selected items; on the right, a clear button to go to booking review. Finally, a light pagination strip anchors the bottom, with centered page numbers and next/previous controls. The entire page reads as a clean series of horizontal bands inside a classic, centered layout, with function-first design and moderate spacing that feels practical and slightly traditional rather than flashy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the main content column that begins directly beneath the site’s global header and full-width navigation bar. It constrains all equipment-hire components to a centered container occupying roughly 80–90% of the viewport width, with a max-width of about 1100–1200px, aligned to a 12-column grid. There is approximately 32px (4 base units) of vertical margin between the bottom of the global navigation and the top of this content container, and around 48px of margin at the bottom before the site footer. Inside this controller, the first band is the page title area: a left-aligned H1 “Equipment Hire” sitting at about 24–32px from the top of the container, spanning roughly the left 8–10 columns, with a shorter subheading directly beneath it, separated by about 8–12px. A subtle horizontal rule then runs nearly the full width of the container, positioned about 16px below the subheading, visually closing the hero-text band. Roughly 24px below this rule, the filter-and-sort toolbar is placed as the next full-width band, followed by the results summary, then the equipment grid, then the booking review toolbar, and finally pagination controls. All these components are stacked vertically with 16–32px gaps, echoing the mockup’s banded structure and preserving a clear top-to-bottom reading order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a standard scrollable content wrapper; all child components flow in normal document order without fixed or sticky behavior. The controller centers its contents and enforces consistent horizontal padding (16–24px) on left and right edges inside the max-width container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-and-sort toolbar forms a strong, slim horizontal band immediately below the title/subheading section and its horizontal rule. It sits about 24px beneath the rule, spanning the full width of the inner content container from left padding to right padding. Vertically, the band is modest in height—around 56–64px total—created by 8–12px of vertical padding and the height of the controls themselves. It uses a slightly darker or tinted background than the page body (similar in visual weight to the mockup’s dark navigation bar) to visually separate it from the content above and below. Within the 12-column grid, the left two-thirds to three-quarters (approximately 8–9 columns) is dedicated to filter controls (e.g., equipment type dropdown, location filter, capacity range), arranged left to right. These controls sit aligned on a single baseline with 16–24px horizontal spacing between them, following the moderate padding pattern described in the spacing system. The rightmost 3–4 columns host sorting and view options (e.g., “Sort by” dropdown and possibly a compact layout toggle), right-aligned to mirror the right-aligned contact info in the mockup’s header. The entire toolbar reads as a functional utility strip that anchors the top of the results, with clean separation above and below (16–24px) so it doesn’t feel cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal document flow beneath the page title; it scrolls away with the page rather than staying fixed, matching the conservative, non-sticky behavior of classic WordPress-style themes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary sits directly below the filter-and-sort toolbar as a lighter, more understated band. It is separated from the toolbar by about 16px of vertical space, preserving the sense of distinct horizontal layers. The summary text spans the full width of the content container but visually occupies mostly the left half to two-thirds (about 6–8 columns), starting from the left padding. It typically contains a single line such as “Showing 12 of 48 pieces of equipment,” possibly followed by a short list of active filter chips. The text has 8–12px of vertical padding above and below, making this band relatively shallow—visually about half the height of the filter toolbar. Optionally, a thin, light top border (or emphasized baseline) can run across the width behind the summary, echoing the subtle horizontal rule used beneath the page title. On larger screens, any secondary actions like “Clear all filters” or a compact filter badge legend can be aligned to the right, occupying the rightmost 4–6 columns and baseline-aligned with the summary text. Overall, this band feels compact and informational, slightly denser than the title area but still open compared to the grid below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows directly under the filter toolbar without special behavior; the summary text wraps responsively within the centered container but is otherwise static.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The equipment list grid is the dominant visual content block and sits about 24–32px below the results summary, occupying the majority of the page’s vertical space. It fills the full width of the inner container, adhering to the 12-column grid. On a typical desktop layout, each equipment card spans 3 columns so that four cards fit per row, closely echoing the mockup’s four-image strip. Narrow horizontal gutters of approximately 16–24px separate the cards, reflecting the desire to maximize visible content while avoiding crowding. Vertically, rows are separated by about 24px to maintain clarity between rows. Each card is a vertical rectangle with a subtle border or light card background, containing a top-aligned equipment image that spans the full width of the card (roughly the upper 40–50% of the card height), and text details (name, category, key specs, pricing, and primary action) stacked beneath with 16–24px of internal padding. The grid can extend for several rows, forming a strong horizontal band of imagery and data in the midsection of the page. The left-to-right scanning of each row, followed by downward movement to subsequent rows, mirrors the eye movement across the four machinery images in the mockup. There is about 32px of clear space at the bottom of the grid before the booking review toolbar, giving a sense of closure to the listing block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard responsive grid behavior within the fixed-width container: items wrap to new rows as needed and reflow to fewer columns on smaller viewports, but remain in normal document flow with no fixed or masonry behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls sit at the very bottom of the main content stack, directly beneath the booking review toolbar, separated from it by about 16–24px of vertical space. They form a shallow horizontal strip that spans the full width of the content container but focuses attention toward the center. A thin top border or subtle horizontal line runs across the container width, visually tying this strip to the horizontal separators used higher on the page. Within this band, the pagination elements (previous/next arrows and page numbers) are horizontally centered, occupying roughly the middle 4–6 columns of the 12-column grid. Vertical padding of about 12–16px above and below the controls ensures they are easy to interact with without feeling heavy. On the far left and far right edges of the band (outer columns), there is typically empty space, maintaining the conservative, uncluttered feel and clearly signaling that pagination is a utility rather than primary content. This strip anchors the end of the listing, signaling that the scrollable content has reached a natural conclusion.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears at the bottom of the page content in normal flow; it does not remain visible when scrolling, consistent with a traditional, non-sticky pagination pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking review toolbar is positioned immediately below the equipment list grid and above the pagination controls, separated from the grid by roughly 24–32px of vertical space so it reads as a distinct band but still visually connected to the listing. It spans the full width of the content container, with a slightly emphasized background—lighter and less dominant than the filter toolbar, but more prominent than the plain page background. Vertically, it is similar in height to the filter bar, around 56–72px including padding. Inside the 12-column grid, the left 4–5 columns are used for a concise text summary such as “2 items selected · Est. daily total $X,” left-aligned and vertically centered. The right 7–8 columns house the primary CTA button (e.g., “Review Booking”) aligned to the right edge, potentially with a small secondary link (like “Clear selection”) to its left. This reproduces the mockup’s service vs. features layout idea—two unequal columns with different functions—while remaining horizontally banded. The toolbar acts as a functional bridge between exploring equipment (the grid above) and advancing in the booking funnel (pagination and further navigation below), clearly visible but not as visually heavy as the top filter band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in normal document flow, scrolling with the page rather than sticking to the bottom of the viewport, reflecting the conservative, non-floating behavior of the reference design. On longer pages, it is encountered naturally as users reach the end of the visible results.

name: Equipment Detail
title: Equipment Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a selected hire equipment item, lets the user select a hire duration, checks whether the daily rate fits their budget, and adds the item to a hire booking before navigating to the booking summary.

core_features: Load and display equipment details (name, category, description, image, specifications, availability, rating) using the equipment ID from the URL and the getEquipmentDetail interface.
Show clear pricing information including daily rate and currency, and optional notes about minimum/maximum hire days based on the equipment data.
Allow the user to enter a desired hire duration (in days), validating against the equipment’s min_hire_days and max_hire_days if provided.
Calculate and display an estimated line total for the selected hire duration based on the equipment’s daily_rate before submission.
Allow the user to enter an optional daily budget and compare it against the equipment’s daily_rate to indicate whether the equipment is within the user’s budget.
Provide an 'Add to Booking' / 'Hire Now' action that calls addEquipmentToHireBooking with the selected equipment ID and hire duration.
Handle success and error states from addEquipmentToHireBooking, displaying confirmation details (including the created/updated booking items) or error messages.
Allow the user, after a successful add to booking, to navigate to the Hire Booking Summary page (booking.html) with the booking identifier in the URL so that the booking can be reviewed there.
Provide navigation back to the equipment hire listing page (equipment_hire.html) for users who wish to continue browsing instead of reviewing the booking immediately.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View equipment details
1. User arrives on equipment_detail.html with an equipment ID in the URL (e.g., a query parameter named for the equipment identifier).
2. The page reads the ID from the URL and invokes getEquipmentDetail with that value.
3. While data is loading, the page shows a loading state; if the call fails, it shows an error state with an option to navigate back to the equipment listing.
4. On success, the page displays the equipment’s core details (name, category, description, image, specifications, availability, rating) and pricing information (daily_rate, currency, min/max hire days if available).

Workflow: Check whether the equipment is within budget
1. After equipment details load, the user can optionally enter their daily budget into a budget input field.
2. As the user updates this budget value, the component compares the entered budget with the equipment’s daily_rate from getEquipmentDetail.
3. If the daily_rate is greater than the entered budget, the page displays a message indicating that the equipment is above the user’s daily budget; if the daily_rate is less than or equal to the budget, the page indicates that it fits within the budget.
4. This budget check does not prevent booking; it is purely informational to support the user’s decision.

Workflow: Select hire duration and view estimated cost
1. The user enters a desired hire duration (in days) into a numeric input field.
2. The component validates the entered value:
   - If min_hire_days is provided by getEquipmentDetail, the value must be greater than or equal to that.
   - If max_hire_days is provided by getEquipmentDetail, the value must be less than or or equal to that.
   - If either boundary is not provided, it is not applied as a constraint.
3. If validation fails, the page displays appropriate validation messages and disables the 'Add to Booking' action until the input is corrected.
4. When the input is valid, the component calculates an estimated line total (daily_rate multiplied by the entered hire_days) and displays it for the user as an estimate prior to calling addEquipmentToHireBooking.

Workflow: Add equipment to hire booking
1. After choosing a valid hire duration, the user clicks the 'Add to Booking' or 'Hire Now' button.
2. The page calls addEquipmentToHireBooking with parameters:
   - equipmentId: the equipment ID read from the URL and associated with the current detail data.
   - hire_days: the validated hire duration input from the user.
3. While the request is in progress, the button is disabled and a submitting state is shown.
4. On success, the page stores the returned booking summary in page state, including booking_id, status, total_days, total_cost, currency, and the array of items (with booking_item_id, equipment_id, equipment_name, daily_rate, hire_days, line_total_cost).
5. The page then displays a confirmation panel summarizing the added item (using the relevant item from the returned items array that matches the current equipment_id) and the updated booking totals.
6. On error, the page shows an error message allowing the user to retry the action.

Workflow: Navigate to booking summary after adding to booking
1. Once addEquipmentToHireBooking succeeds and a booking_id is available in the response, the confirmation panel exposes a 'Review Booking' button.
2. When the user clicks 'Review Booking', the page navigates to booking.html and passes the booking identifier as a URL parameter so that the Hire Booking Summary page can load the correct booking.
3. The confirmation panel also exposes a 'Continue Browsing Hire' option that navigates back to equipment_hire.html, letting the user add more equipment before reviewing the booking.

Workflow: Handle unavailable equipment
1. After getEquipmentDetail returns data, the page checks the is_available property.
2. If is_available indicates that the equipment cannot currently be hired, the hire duration form and the 'Add to Booking' button are disabled or hidden, and an availability message is displayed.
3. The user still can read specifications and navigate back to the hire listing page.
interactions: On page load, read the equipment ID from the URL query parameters and invoke getEquipmentDetail with that ID.
If the equipment ID is missing or invalid, show an error or not-found state and provide a link back to equipment_hire.html.
When getEquipmentDetail is pending, show a loading indicator; on success, bind all returned fields to their respective display components; on error, show an actionable error message.
When the user edits the hire duration input, validate the value against min_hire_days and max_hire_days and update the estimated line total in real time.
When the user edits the optional daily budget input, compare it against the loaded daily_rate and update a budget status indicator in real time.

When the user clicks 'Add to Booking' / 'Hire Now', validate that the equipment is available and the hire duration is valid, then call addEquipmentToHireBooking with equipmentId and hire_days; handle loading, success, and error states.
After a successful addEquipmentToHireBooking call, update the booking confirmation panel with the returned booking and item data and expose 'Review Booking' and 'Continue Browsing' actions.
When the user clicks 'Review Booking', navigate to booking.html with the booking_id from the addEquipmentToHireBooking response as a URL parameter.
When the user clicks 'Continue Browsing Hire', navigate to equipment_hire.html.
When the user clicks any back-to-list link or button in the header, navigate to equipment_hire.html without modifying booking state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state driver is the equipment ID passed via the URL to equipment_detail.html. On initialisation and whenever the URL changes, the page reads this ID from the query parameters and passes it to getEquipmentDetail. If the ID is absent or invalid, the page shows an error/not-found state and offers navigation back to the hire listing. The page stores the result of getEquipmentDetail in its in-memory state as the current Equipment entity, which is then used across components (summary, pricing, availability, and hire form).

The page maintains additional transient state values: (1) the current hire_days input, (2) validation flags related to hire_days (e.g., below min_hire_days, above max_hire_days, or non-numeric), (3) an optional user-entered daily budget, and (4) the result of the most recent addEquipmentToHireBooking call (including booking_id, totals, and items). All of these states are held in memory only for the lifetime of the page view and are not persisted in localStorage or similar.

When addEquipmentToHireBooking returns successfully, the page updates its booking-related state with the returned HireBooking and HireBookingItem data. This state is used to render the confirmation panel and to provide the booking_id needed for subsequent navigation. The booking_id from the response is then passed via URL parameter when navigating to booking.html so that the Hire Booking Summary page can load the correct booking. No booking information is encoded directly into the URL beyond the booking identifier, and no booking-related state is stored outside the current page context.

components.functionality: Reads the equipment identifier from the URL, calls getEquipmentDetail with that identifier, and exposes loading, success, and error states along with the loaded Equipment data to other components.

Displays high-level equipment details such as name, category, description, average_rating, rating_count, image_url, and a simple availability status based on is_available. Provides navigation back to the equipment hire listing.
Shows the equipment’s specifications text and any extended description information for users to confirm technical suitability.

Displays the equipment’s daily_rate and currency, along with any min_hire_days and max_hire_days information. Provides an optional daily budget input that the user can enter, and compares this input against the daily_rate to indicate whether the equipment’s daily cost is within the user’s budget.

Allows the user to enter the desired hire_days for the current equipment. Validates the input against min_hire_days and max_hire_days from the Equipment data if they are present. Calculates and displays an estimated line total using daily_rate multiplied by the input hire_days. Enables the 'Add to Booking' / 'Hire Now' button only when the equipment is available and the hire_days input is valid.

Implements the call to addEquipmentToHireBooking when the user submits a valid hire duration. Uses the current equipment ID (from URL and Equipment data) and the validated hire_days as parameters. Updates shared booking state with the returned booking_id, status, total_days, total_cost, currency, and items so that the confirmation panel and navigation controls can use this data.

After a successful addEquipmentToHireBooking call, displays confirmation information for the current equipment line item and the overall booking totals. Identifies the relevant HireBookingItem by matching equipment_id from the response to the current Equipment.id, and displays fields such as equipment_name, daily_rate, hire_days, and line_total_cost along with booking-level total_days and total_cost.

Provides post-booking navigation actions. Uses the booking_id from the most recent addEquipmentToHireBooking response to construct a URL for booking.html with the booking identifier as a query parameter for the 'Review Booking' button. Also provides a 'Continue Browsing Hire' action that navigates back to equipment_hire.html.

Aggregates and displays availability and validation messages. Shows an informational or blocking message if is_available is false. Shows validation errors for invalid hire_days input (such as violating min_hire_days or max_hire_days constraints) and any errors returned from addEquipmentToHireBooking.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop-oriented grid typical of classic WordPress themes, with the main content constrained to a centered max-width container and internal content (like the 4 images) snapping to equal fractional columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow inside a centered max-width content container (approximately 960–1140px wide). Immediately below the site’s global header/navigation, the equipment-detail area begins with a summary header band (title, key info, and images) that mimics the hero section from the mockup. Below this, content splits into two unequal columns: a narrower left column for specifications and a wider right column for pricing, budget, hire duration, and actions, similar to the 'SERVICE' vs 'FEATURES & APPLICATIONS' pattern. Confirmation and navigation controls then appear as separate horizontal bands stacked beneath the columns.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow inside a centered max-width content container (approximately 960–1140px wide). Immediately below the site’s global header/navigation, the equipment-detail area begins with a summary header band (title, key info, and images) that mimics the hero section from the mockup. Below this, content splits into two unequal columns: a narrower left column for specifications and a wider right column for pricing, budget, hire duration, and actions, similar to the 'SERVICE' vs 'FEATURES & APPLICATIONS' pattern. Confirmation and navigation controls then appear as separate horizontal bands stacked beneath the columns.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The summary header occupies a moderate vertical slice with comfortable whitespace (24–32px padding top and bottom). Within it, the H1 and intro text sit above a low-profile horizontal rule. A compact row of equipment imagery or key badges can sit below the text, spanning most of the content width. The two-column section below uses about 35–40% of the width for the left specifications panel and 60–65% for the right pricing/booking stack. Vertical gaps between major bands (summary header, columns, confirmation, navigation) follow the 24–40px spacing from the mockup, while internal component padding stays in the 16–24px range.
layout_strategies.content_density: The top summary header is relatively open with a content-first, uncluttered feel.
