As the user moves down into the two-column area, information density increases: the left column uses tight but readable bullet lists, and the right column stacks pricing, budget calculations, form fields, and feedback messages in a slightly denser, functional pattern. The confirmation panel is moderately dense but visually grouped, and navigation controls remain lightweight. Overall, the density ramps up toward the decision/action region in the right column, echoing the mockup’s denser lower sections.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the top-left with the equipment name (H1), moves down through any short description and across a horizontal strip of equipment imagery or key attributes. From there, it drops into the two-column zone, scanning the left specifications list first, and then naturally shifting to the wider, more visually prominent right column where pricing, budget check, hire duration, and the 'Add to Booking' button reside. After completing the action, attention moves downward to the confirmation summary band and finally to the navigation controls at the bottom. Feedback messages appear immediately below the form where the user is focused, preserving a direct cause-and-effect flow.

layout.overall_description: Within a centered, desktop-oriented content container, the Equipment Detail page presents itself as a series of horizontal bands reminiscent of a classic corporate WordPress layout. A clean summary header at the top introduces the equipment with a left-aligned title, short description, and a subtle horizontal rule, followed by a compact row of imagery or key stats that stretches across the container. Beneath this, the screen resolves into two uneven columns: a narrower left column listing specifications in bullet form and a wider right column hosting pricing, budget evaluation, hire duration controls, and the primary booking action. All panels sit inside simple, flat boxes with light borders or subtle background shifts, separated by 24–32px of vertical space. After an item is added, a full-width confirmation strip appears below the columns, reinforcing the outcome before the user encounters the bottom band of navigation controls that guide them back to browsing or forward to the booking summary. The overall impression is structured, conservative, and functional, with clear vertical rhythm and a left-to-right, top-to-bottom reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a semi-transparent overlay positioned directly above the entire equipment detail content area (from the top of the equipment-summary-header down to the bottom of the booking-navigation-controls). It spans 100% of the width of the main content container and 100% of its height, dimming the underlying bands without obscuring their structure. In the center of this overlay, horizontally and vertically centered, sits a small spinner or simple loading indicator with a short status message. The overlay respects the max-width of the main container, aligning with its left and right edges, so it feels like a layer within the page rather than a full-viewport takeover.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolute-positioned overlay within the main content container; it covers and disables interaction with all underlying components while data is loading, but scroll position remains unchanged.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The equipment-summary-header sits at the very top of the equipment detail area, directly beneath the site’s global navigation band. It spans the full width of the centered content container (approximately 80–90% of the viewport width on desktop) with generous padding of about 24px on all sides. The equipment name is left-aligned at the top of this panel as a prominent H1, with a one- or two-line subheading or short descriptive text directly below, separated by roughly 8–12px. There is ample white space (about 24px) between the text block and a subtle horizontal rule that runs nearly the full width of the panel. Directly below the rule, occupying the lower third of this header band, is a single row of 3–4 rectangular equipment images or key attribute tiles aligned left to right, each taking up an equal fraction of the content width with narrow 8–12px gaps between them. This row collectively spans most of the header’s width but leaves small left and right interior padding consistent with the panel. The bottom of this header panel is separated from the two-column section below by a vertical margin of around 32px.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level band at the top of the main content; it scrolls with the page and sets the visual context for all subsequent panels.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The equipment-specifications-panel begins directly below the equipment-summary-header, aligned to the left side of the main content container. It forms the left column of the primary content area, occupying roughly 35–40% of the container’s width. Its top edge aligns horizontally with the top of the equipment-pricing-and-budget-panel on the right, creating a clean two-column band. The panel itself is a vertically oriented box with 16–24px of internal padding, possibly outlined by a light border or slightly tinted background to distinguish it subtly from the page background. A section heading such as 'Specifications' is left-aligned at the top, followed by a vertical stack of bullet-pointed specification items. The bullets are mildly dense, with 4–8px between lines and 12–16px between logical groups, giving this panel a text-heavy but organized feel. The panel’s content can extend downward beyond the height of the adjacent right-side panels if needed, with approximately 24px of margin below before the next full-width band (such as the booking-confirmation-panel).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static left column within the two-column layout; its width remains fixed relative to the main container on desktop, and it stacks above the right column on narrow viewports if responsiveness is implemented.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The equipment-pricing-and-budget-panel sits to the right of the equipment-specifications-panel, forming the topmost element of the wider right column. It occupies approximately 60–65% of the main content container’s width and aligns its top edge horizontally with the top of the specifications panel, maintaining a consistent vertical band. The panel is given slightly stronger visual weight than the specs panel—such as a light grey background fill or a more defined border—while still adhering to the flat, corporate style. Inside, 16–24px padding frames the content. Pricing information (daily rate, possible minimum hire, etc.) is left-aligned at the top, with key numbers visually emphasized via slightly larger or bolder text. Directly beneath, separated by about 16px, a compact budget-check subsection presents inputs or a short summary indicating whether the daily rate fits the user’s specified budget. Text is kept moderately dense but clearly structured with subtle subheadings or small labels. The bottom of this panel sits about 16px above the hire-duration-form, creating a tight vertical relationship that visually groups all booking-related controls in the right column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static right-column panel; it anchors the interactive booking area and stays vertically stacked above the hire-duration-form within the right column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The hire-duration-form is placed directly below the equipment-pricing-and-budget-panel in the same right-side column, maintaining the same width (60–65% of the container) and horizontal alignment. A vertical gap of approximately 16px separates its top edge from the bottom of the pricing panel, making the connection between pricing and duration clear but visually distinct. The form is relatively shallow in height, with fields arranged in a simple vertical stack: for example, start date, end date, or number of days, each on its own line. Internal padding of around 16–20px ensures that labels and fields are not cramped, following the moderate-density feel of the mockup’s lower content sections. Field labels are left-aligned above or beside input controls, and there is about 12–16px spacing between individual form rows. The bottom of the form ends about 8–12px above the availability-and-validation-messages, so that any feedback appears tightly coupled to user input.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the right column; it expands vertically as validation hints or helper text wrap, but it maintains its position between the pricing panel above and the feedback/messages and action button below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-booking-action manifests visually as a horizontal button row at the bottom of the right column’s booking stack. It sits directly below the availability-and-validation-messages, separated by about 16–20px of vertical space to create a clear action zone. The row spans the full width of the right column but keeps the primary 'Add to Booking' button right-aligned, with any secondary actions (like 'Cancel' or 'Back') placed to its left with 8–16px horizontal spacing. The button height is consistent with the design’s component padding (about 12–16px vertical padding inside the button) and stands out through a solid fill color while still fitting the conservative corporate theme (no gradients, minimal rounding). The area above and below the buttons is not overly spacious, making this section feel action-focused and slightly denser than the more open summary header at the top.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static action row anchored at the bottom of the right column; when the page scrolls, it moves with the rest of the content and is not sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-confirmation-panel appears beneath the entire two-column section, forming a new full-width band inside the main content container. Its top edge is separated from the bottom of the taller of the two columns (specifications or right column) by approximately 32px, clearly indicating it as a subsequent step. This panel spans 100% of the container width and uses 24px of internal padding. It may adopt a light tinted background or a subtle border to differentiate it from the general page background, suggesting a message or summary state. Inside, key details of the added booking—equipment name, hire dates, calculated cost—are arranged in a simple, left-aligned vertical layout or a light two-column grid, but with less density than the specs panel to keep it easily scannable. A brief confirmation message is placed at the top of the panel, with supporting details underneath. The bottom of this panel is set about 24–32px above the booking-navigation-controls band, maintaining the page’s clear section banding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width band that appears conditionally after a successful add-to-booking action; it pushes subsequent content downward as it becomes visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-navigation-controls occupy the final band of the equipment detail page’s main content, directly beneath the booking-confirmation-panel (or, if no confirmation is present, beneath the two-column section). A vertical margin of roughly 24–32px separates its top from the content above, aligning with the section spacing established in the rest of the page. This band is shallow in height, spanning the full width of the content container with about 16–20px of vertical padding. Within it, navigation elements are arranged along a single horizontal line: typically, a secondary 'Back to Equipment List' link or button aligned to the left, and a more prominent 'Go to Booking Summary' button aligned to the right. The space between left and right controls is largely empty, reinforcing the horizontal banding and guiding the user’s eye from one end to the other. The styling remains flat and understated, matching the conservative theme while still clearly indicating clickable controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static bottom-of-section navigation band; it scrolls off the screen with the rest of the content and is not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability-and-validation-messages panel is tightly integrated into the right column, sitting directly beneath the hire-duration-form and above the add-to-booking-action. It spans the full width of the right column and is separated from the form by a small vertical gap of around 8–12px, reinforcing its role as immediate feedback on the user’s recent input. When inactive, this area is minimal or collapsed, preserving vertical space. When messages are present, they appear as one or more horizontally spanning lines or compact boxes with left-aligned text, possibly framed by a light colored border or background tone to indicate status (e.g., green for available, amber/red for issues) while still respecting the restrained visual style. Vertical padding of about 8–12px inside each message keeps them legible but not overly airy, contributing to the slightly denser feel of this lower-right interaction zone. The bottom of the message area sits approximately 16–20px above the add-to-booking-action button row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Dynamic inline panel within the right column; it expands and contracts based on the presence of messages but maintains its relative position between the hire-duration-form and the add-to-booking-action.

name: Hire Booking Summary
title: Hire Booking Summary
description: Review selected hire equipment, adjust hire durations, enter booking details, and submit a hire booking request for processing.

core_features: Load the current hire booking summary using getHireBookingSummary, including all hire items, daily rates, hire durations, totals, and any existing contact details.
Display each hire item in a structured list with equipment name, daily rate, current hire days, and calculated line total cost.
Allow inline adjustment of hire duration (hire_days) per item and update booking totals by calling updateHireBookingItemDuration.
Show aggregated booking totals (total_days, total_cost, currency) for all items in the booking.
Provide a booking details form that collects or edits site_postcode, contact_name, contact_email, and contact_phone, prefilled when available from getHireBookingSummary.
Validate booking details on the client side before submission (e.g., required fields present, basic email/phone format checks).
Submit the booking request using submitHireBooking and surface the returned status, success flag, and message to the user.
Lock down editing (durations and contact fields) when the booking status is no longer in a draft-like state (e.g., submitted or confirmed), based on the status returned from the APIs.
Provide navigation back to equipment_hire.html via a 'Continue browsing equipment' action.
After a successful submission, provide a 'Contact us about this booking' link that navigates to contact.html with enquiry_type and subject URL parameters set for a booking follow-up.
user_workflows: 1. User lands on booking.html (optionally with a from URL parameter indicating the source page). The page initializes, reads the from parameter for context, and calls getHireBookingSummary. The returned data populates the booking items list, totals, and any saved contact details.

2. The user reviews the list of hire items. For any item, the user adjusts the hire duration (hire_days) in an inline control. When the value is changed and confirmed (e.g., on blur or explicit update action), the page validates that the value is a positive integer and then calls updateHireBookingItemDuration with the corresponding booking_item_id and hire_days. On a successful response, the page re-calls getHireBookingSummary to refresh item details and booking totals.
3. The user reviews the updated booking totals (total_days, total_cost, currency) displayed in a summary panel. The user can repeat step 2 for multiple items until satisfied with the configuration.

4. The user enters or edits site_postcode, contact_name, contact_email, and contact_phone in the booking details form. If getHireBookingSummary provided existing values, they appear prefilled and can be adjusted.

5. The user clicks the 'Confirm Booking Request' primary action. The form performs client-side validation; if any required fields are missing or invalid, the form shows validation errors and blocks submission. If validation passes, the page calls submitHireBooking with the current site_postcode, contact_name, contact_email, and contact_phone values.

6. After submitHireBooking resolves, the page reads success, status, message, and booking_id from the response. It displays the server-provided message and updates the local booking status. If success is true, the page disables further editing of hire durations and contact details, highlights the updated booking status, and reveals a 'Contact us about this booking' link to contact.html with enquiry_type and subject URL parameters prefilled for a booking follow-up.
7. At any point before or after submission, the user can click 'Continue browsing equipment' to navigate to equipment_hire.html to add more items or explore other equipment.

8. After submission, if the user wants to discuss the booking, they click 'Contact us about this booking'. The page navigates to contact.html with URL parameters enquiry_type=general_enquiry and subject=Equipment hire booking follow-up so the contact form is preconfigured for a booking-related enquiry.
interactions: On page load, the page reads the from URL parameter (if present) and uses it to adjust contextual text (e.g., link labels or explanatory notes) without altering core functionality. Then it automatically calls getHireBookingSummary and stores the returned booking object in page state.

When getHireBookingSummary returns, the booking items are bound to the booking items list component, and the booking-level fields (status, site_postcode, contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, total_days, total_cost, currency) are bound to the totals and form components.

When the user changes a hire_days control for a specific booking item, the component validates the input locally (e.g., non-empty, numeric, > 0). On valid change confirmation, it triggers updateHireBookingItemDuration with booking_item_id and the new hire_days. While awaiting the response, the item or totals panel can enter a 'pending' state. After the API responds successfully, the page calls getHireBookingSummary again to refresh items and totals. On failure, the component surfaces an error state (e.g., inline error sourced from the API error, not hardcoded) and reverts or holds the previous value.

If getHireBookingSummary returns a booking with no items, the items list component shows an empty-state view and exposes the 'Continue browsing equipment' navigation action. The totals and confirm actions are hidden or disabled until at least one item is present.

The booking contact details form fields are two-way bound to the HireBooking-level fields in the page state. As the user types, local state is updated. When the user clicks 'Confirm Booking Request', the form's onSubmit handler checks each field against validation rules and only then calls submitHireBooking.

On submitHireBooking success, the page updates the booking status in local state using the status returned by the interface, displays the server-provided message, and switches the UI to a read-only mode for hire durations and contact details. The 'Confirm Booking Request' button is disabled or hidden, and the 'Contact us about this booking' link becomes visible.

On submitHireBooking failure, the page displays the server-provided message in an error-status component and leaves the form and items editable so the user can correct any issues and retry submission.

Clicking 'Continue browsing equipment' triggers navigation to equipment_hire.html (no additional parameters), leaving booking state to be maintained by the backend/session as defined outside this page.

Clicking 'Contact us about this booking' after a successful submission triggers navigation to contact.html with URL parameters enquiry_type=general_enquiry and subject=Equipment hire booking follow-up appended to the URL. The booking_id is not added as a parameter by this page unless defined elsewhere in the system.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary URL parameter for this page is 'from'. On initial load of booking.html, the page parses window.location.search to extract the optional 'from' parameter. This parameter is not sent to any interface but is stored in local page state and used solely to control contextual messaging or the labeling of navigation links (for example, differentiating whether the user arrived from an equipment detail page or the general equipment hire list). After handling the 'from' parameter, the page calls getHireBookingSummary without additional parameters. The response is stored in in-memory state as the current booking context, including booking_id, status, contact fields, totals, currency, and items. The page uses the booking status from this state to determine whether editing is permitted: if the status equates to a draft-like state (derived directly from the returned status value), item duration fields and contact fields remain editable; if the status indicates a submitted or confirmed state, these controls are set to read-only and the submission button is disabled. When a user changes an item's hire_days, the new value is passed to updateHireBookingItemDuration along with booking_item_id. After receiving a response, the page re-invokes getHireBookingSummary to reload the full booking snapshot so item-level values and totals remain consistent with server calculations. The booking_id returned by these interfaces is kept in memory for internal use (e.g., display, logic) but is not written to the URL by this page. Similarly, the result of submitHireBooking (booking_id, status, success, message) updates the same booking state object; success and status drive the visibility and enabled/disabled state of buttons and fields, while message is displayed to the user. Navigation away from the page (to equipment_hire.html or contact.html) relies solely on standard URL navigation with the specified query parameters and does not use localStorage or any other client-side persistence mechanism.

components.functionality: On initial render, parse the 'from' URL parameter for contextual use and invoke getHireBookingSummary to retrieve the current HireBooking and its associated HireBookingItems. Store the response in page-level state for use by all other components.

Render the list of hire items from the current booking, including equipment_name, daily_rate, hire_days, and line_total_cost. Provide per-item controls for adjusting hire_days when the booking status allows editing. Handle empty-state behavior when there are no items.

For each HireBookingItem, provide an input control bound to hire_days. Validate the new value locally and, when the user confirms the change, call updateHireBookingItemDuration with the item's booking_item_id and updated hire_days. On success, request a booking refresh via the init loader. On failure, expose the error state and revert or retain the previous hire_days as appropriate.

Display aggregated booking totals including total_days, total_cost, and currency from the current HireBooking. Reactively update when the booking state changes (e.g., after updateHireBookingItemDuration completes and getHireBookingSummary is called again).

Provide a form for entering and editing booking details: site_postcode, contact_name, contact_email, and contact_phone. Prefill fields from HireBooking when available, perform client-side validation, and on valid submission call submitHireBooking. Disable editing when the booking status indicates the booking has already been submitted or confirmed.

Display the outcome of the submitHireBooking call using the returned success, status, and message values. Show success or error states accordingly and update the page to reflect the new booking status.

Provide page-level actions such as the 'Confirm Booking Request' button, the 'Continue browsing equipment' link, and after successful submission, the 'Contact us about this booking' link. Control visibility and enabled state of these actions based on the current booking status and submission result.

Use the 'from' URL parameter stored in page state to adjust contextual messaging (for example, indicating whether the user came from a specific equipment details page or the general equipment hire list) without affecting core functionality or API calls.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop-oriented grid typical of classic WordPress themes, with the main content constrained to a centered max-width container and internal content (like the 4 images) snapping to equal fractional columns.
layout.layout_pattern: All booking components sit inside the same centered main content container used across the site, directly beneath the global header and dark navigation bar. Within that container, content is arranged in vertical bands: first the booking context/heading, then a horizontal rule, then an optional status banner, followed by a two-column booking summary area (items on the left, totals on the right), then a full-width contact/booking details form, and finally a bottom actions bar. The booking duration controls live inside each booking item row rather than as a separate standalone band, keeping the top-to-bottom flow clean and linear.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All booking components sit inside the same centered main content container used across the site, directly beneath the global header and dark navigation bar. Within that container, content is arranged in vertical bands: first the booking context/heading, then a horizontal rule, then an optional status banner, followed by a two-column booking summary area (items on the left, totals on the right), then a full-width contact/booking details form, and finally a bottom actions bar. The booking duration controls live inside each booking item row rather than as a separate standalone band, keeping the top-to-bottom flow clean and linear.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content container spans roughly 80–90% of the viewport width up to a max-width typical of a 12-column grid (around 1040–1200px), centered on the page. The upper context area (title and step information) uses moderate vertical space with comfortable white space above and below. The booking summary area divides the grid into an 8-column left region for the booking items list (about two-thirds of the content width) and a 4-column right region for the totals panel (about one-third). The contact form occupies the full width of the container in a single band. The actions bar forms a shallow horizontal strip beneath the form. Vertical gaps between these bands follow the 24–32px spacing rhythm observed in the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: The upper context area is relatively open: a left-aligned heading, subtext, and a rule with generous breathing room. Density increases in the booking items and totals region, similar to the SERVICE/FEATURES columns in the mockup: item rows, inline controls, and monetary breakdowns create a slightly dense but organized block. The contact form maintains that moderate density with clearly separated form groups and labels. The actions bar is compact but not cramped, with enough padding around buttons to keep it usable and visually clear.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first catches the booking context indicator at the top of the content container, reading left-aligned heading and any step label. It then naturally drops to the subtle horizontal rule and any status banner beneath. From there, attention moves down into the main two-column content: scanning the list of selected hire items on the left, including inline duration controls, and glancing right to check totals and key summary metrics. After reviewing items and totals, the eye continues downward to the full-width contact details form to enter booking information, then finally to the bottom actions bar where the primary submit and secondary navigation actions reside. The loader, when active, temporarily overrides this flow by placing a centered indicator in front of all other content.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing site header and dark navigation bar, the page transitions into a centered, content-first booking summary layout that echoes an older but clean corporate WordPress theme. A single main column is visually anchored at the center of the page, with the 'Hire Booking Summary' context indicator and step information sitting at the very top of this column, left-aligned and surrounded by comfortable vertical whitespace. A thin horizontal rule spans the width of the content container, quietly separating this context area from the functional booking content. Immediately below, an optional status banner forms a shallow, full-width strip used to confirm successful submissions or display errors. The core of the page is a two-column band: on the left, a vertically stacked list of booking items that consumes roughly two-thirds of the container, each row showing equipment details and an inline duration editor on the right side of the row; on the right, a vertically aligned totals panel occupying the remaining one-third width, summarizing hire costs and key figures. Beneath this summary band, the layout returns to a single-column structure where the contact details form stretches across the full container width, grouping fields into logical rows and columns but maintaining the conservative styling. At the bottom, a horizontally oriented actions bar spans the content width, with secondary actions on the left and a prominent submit button group on the right, forming the final call-to-action band. When data is being fetched or submitted, a semi-transparent loader overlay rests above this entire content stack, dimming it and drawing focus to a centered loading indicator, but otherwise the page presents a straightforward, vertically-scrolling, banded layout with clear left-to-right scanning points.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The initial loader appears as a semi-transparent overlay that covers the entire main content container, from just below the site’s navigation bar down to the end of the booking content. It aligns with the same centered max-width column as the rest of the content, respecting the left and right bounds of that container rather than spanning edge-to-edge across the full viewport. Within this overlay, a compact loading indicator (spinner and brief status text) is centered both horizontally and vertically, creating a focal point in the middle of the visible content area. Underneath, the underlying components (context indicator, items list, totals, form, actions bar) remain laid out in their normal positions but are visually muted by the overlay. Vertical spacing and layout beneath are unchanged; the overlay simply floats above them while loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as an absolutely or fixed-position overlay attached to the main content container, covering all booking-related components while active. It scrolls with the page if the content container scrolls but remains visually on top due to a higher stacking order (z-index). Hidden entirely when not loading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking context indicator sits at the very top of the booking page content, directly beneath the global navigation bar. It is constrained to the centered main content container and spans the full width of that container. The primary heading ‘Hire Booking Summary’ (or equivalent) is left-aligned and occupies the upper-left portion of this band, with any supporting text (e.g., brief description or step indicator like ‘Step 2 of 3 – Review & Confirm’) placed immediately below it, also left-aligned and not stretching much wider than the heading text. The vertical space of this band is moderate, using approximately 24–32px of padding above the heading and 16–24px below the supporting text before the next element. Below the text, a subtle horizontal rule extends almost the full width of the content container, visually echoing the mockup’s break between hero text and subsequent content. Overall, this component occupies roughly the top 10–15% of the booking page’s visible height on desktop, mirroring the open, hero-like text band from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level component anchored at the top of the main content column, scrolling away naturally as the user scrolls down the page. No sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission feedback component appears directly below the horizontal rule of the booking context indicator, within the same centered content container. When visible, it forms a shallow, full-width banner-like strip, matching the width of the main column and sitting flush against the left and right edges of that container. Its vertical height is modest—roughly the height of one to two lines of text plus 8–16px of padding above and below—so it reads as a concise status message band rather than a large hero area. Success, warning, or error states can be communicated via background color and icon, but the band maintains the conservative, flat styling consistent with the rest of the page. There is a small 16–24px vertical gap below it before the booking items and totals area begins, ensuring it is visually separated yet clearly associated with the overall booking summary context above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow of the document; it appears and disappears between the context indicator and the booking summary area without affecting the positions of components above. When hidden, the content below moves up to occupy the space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking items list forms the left column of the main booking summary section, positioned directly below the submission feedback (or immediately below the context indicator if no feedback is present). This section is laid out using the 12-column grid: the items list occupies roughly 8 of 12 columns, beginning at the left edge of the content container, which translates to about 65–70% of the available width. There is a vertical band title or subtle label aligned to the top-left of this area (e.g., ‘Selected Hire Items’), followed by a stack of item rows. Each item row is a horizontally-oriented card spanning the full width of this left column, with a moderate height: a thumbnail or icon on the far left, text details (equipment name, configuration, rate) in the middle, and the duration control anchored on the far right of the row. Rows have consistent internal padding (around 16px) and are separated by 8–16px vertical gaps, giving a slightly dense but readable list akin to the multi-bullet text sections in the mockup. The entire items list column extends vertically as needed based on the number of items, aligning its top edge with the top of the totals panel on the right to form a coherent two-column band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow, forming a left-aligned column in a responsive two-column layout. On narrower viewports, this column can stack above the totals panel, maintaining its full-width left alignment while preserving vertical spacing between sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The duration control is embedded inside each row of the booking items list rather than occupying its own standalone region. Within a given item card, it is positioned on the far right side, aligned vertically to the middle of the card’s content area. It typically occupies about 20–25% of the item row’s width, acting as a compact control cluster that may include a numeric input, unit label (e.g., ‘days’), and increment/decrement buttons. This cluster is visually separated from the item’s descriptive text by a small 8–16px horizontal gap, but remains on the same horizontal baseline to preserve a clean row structure. In the overall hierarchy of the page, these controls appear in a left-to-right sequence within each item: item thumbnail or icon on the left, item details in the center, duration control on the right, mirroring the mockup’s preference for content running from left descriptive information to right-side actionable or numerical info. Vertical space above and below each duration control is defined by the height of its parent row; no extra row-level padding is added specifically for it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within each booking item row, aligned using the internal grid of the row rather than the page-level grid. It moves with its parent item row when the list scrolls and does not have any sticky or floating behavior of its own.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The totals panel occupies the right-hand column of the main booking summary area, aligned to the top of the booking items list and within the same horizontal band. Using the 12-column grid, it spans roughly 4 of 12 columns on the right, corresponding to about 30–35% of the content container width. There is a narrow horizontal gap (about 24–32px) separating it from the items list on the left, ensuring clear column separation while maintaining a cohesive two-column block similar in feel to the 'SERVICE' vs. 'FEATURES & APPLICATIONS' layout in the mockup. Within the panel, content is arranged in stacked blocks: a heading (e.g., ‘Hire Summary’), followed by line-items such as subtotal, GST, and total, and any key summary notes. Each line item is laid out with labels left-aligned and monetary values right-aligned within the panel, creating a clear scan line for totals. The panel’s internal padding is moderate (around 16–24px on all sides), making it feel like a defined box without heavy borders or shadows, staying consistent with the flat, conservative aesthetic. Vertically, the panel may be shorter than the items list; it simply aligns its top edge with the top of the list and ends where its content ends, leaving white space beneath if the items list is taller.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static right-aligned column within the main grid. On narrower screens or smaller breakpoints, it stacks below the items list, keeping its full-width alignment and preserving top and bottom padding to maintain its identity as a summary panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact details form sits directly beneath the two-column booking summary band, separated from it by a clear vertical margin of approximately 24–32px. It spans the full width of the main content container, returning the layout to a single-column band that echoes the lower text sections of the mockup. At its top-left, a form section heading (e.g., ‘Booking & Contact Details’) is left-aligned, with an optional one-line description below it. The form fields are then laid out in logical groups, typically using an internal two-column structure aligned to the same 12-column grid: for example, name and company fields might appear side-by-side, each taking about half the available width, while longer fields like address or notes span the full width. Vertical spacing between form rows follows the established scale (about 16–24px), making this section moderately dense but not cramped. Left and right padding within the form content aligns with the container edges, avoiding any extra inset that would break the grid continuity. Overall, this block is visually substantial, occupying a significant vertical portion of the page beneath the item summary, similar to a detailed descriptive section in the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width band within the content container, scrolling naturally as the user moves down the page. No sticky or floating behavior; all fields remain within the main document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The actions bar is the final band in the booking page’s main content column, placed directly beneath the contact details form with a vertical gap of about 24px. It spans the full width of the centered container, forming a shallow horizontal strip reminiscent of a simple footer-like control area. Within this bar, actions are arranged with a clear left-right structure: secondary navigation actions (such as ‘Back to Equipment’ or ‘Modify Selection’) are aligned to the left, while the primary action group (e.g., ‘Submit Booking Request’) is aligned to the right. Buttons have consistent horizontal padding (around 16–24px each) and modest vertical padding (8–12px), with 8–16px gaps between them, following the navigation item spacing seen in the mockup. The bar uses enough vertical padding (12–16px) above and below the controls to feel distinct from the form above but remains relatively compact to signal that it is a control band rather than another content section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky control band at the bottom of the main content area. It scrolls normally with the page and does not remain fixed to the viewport edge, preserving the conservative, non-interfering layout style from the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: DUPLICATE_ID_PLACEHOLDER
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static

name: Projects & Case Studies
title: Projects & Case Studies
description: Interactive listing of completed projects and case studies with filter, pagination, and shortlist/compare functionality.

core_features: Load available project filter options (project types, locations, completion year range) via getProjectFilterOptions and initialise the filter controls.
Read URL parameters for project_type, location_region, completion_year_from, completion_year_to, and page to initialise page state and pre-apply filters on load.
Retrieve a paginated list of projects and case studies based on current filters and pagination state via listProjects.
Display projects in a list or grid, showing key fields (title, project_type, location_city, location_region, completion_year, summary, featured_image_url, is_shortlisted).
Allow users to change filters (project type, location, and completion year range), update the URL parameters accordingly, and refresh the project list without using localStorage.
Provide pagination controls that update the page URL parameter and reload the project list using listProjects.
Enable users to add/remove projects to/from a shortlist via addProjectToShortlist from each project card.
Maintain and expose the current shortlist context (shortlist_id and project_ids) on the page so the user can navigate to the comparison page.

Provide a 'View Shortlist' / 'Compare Projects' action that navigates to project_shortlist.html with sufficient identification (e.g., shortlist_id via URL parameter) to load the shortlisted projects there.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Arrive with pre-applied filters from another page
1. User clicks a link on another page that navigates to projects.html with project_type and/or location_region URL parameters.
2. The Projects & Case Studies page reads these URL parameters on load.
3. The page calls getProjectFilterOptions to obtain valid filter values and the completion year range.
4. The filter controls are initialised, matching the project_type and location_region from the URL where possible.
5. The page derives completion_year_from and completion_year_to from URL parameters if present, validating them against the min/max years returned by getProjectFilterOptions.
6. The page determines the current page index from the page URL parameter (or defaults to the first page if not present).
7. The page calls listProjects with the current filter values and page index.
8. The projects list is rendered with cards, each indicating whether it is already shortlisted via the is_shortlisted field.
9. The user may refine filters or change page; the URL and project list update accordingly.

Workflow 2: Manually filtering projects and using pagination
1. User arrives on projects.html without any URL parameters or after a reset action.
2. The page calls getProjectFilterOptions and initialises filter controls with available project types, locations, and the global completion_year_range.
3. The page initialises filter state with no specific project_type/location filter and a completion year range covering the full range from getProjectFilterOptions.
4. The page calls listProjects with current filters and an initial page index.
5. The user changes one or more filters (for example, selects a project_type and a location_region and adjusts the completion year range).
6. On filter submission, the page sets page to the first page, updates the URL query string to reflect the selected project_type, location_region, completion_year_from, and completion_year_to, and then calls listProjects with these values.
7. The user clicks a pagination control (e.g., Next or a specific page number).
8. The pagination component updates the page parameter in the URL and triggers listProjects with the same filters but updated page index.
9. The project list updates to show results for the newly selected page, and the scroll position may be reset to the top of the listing area.

Workflow 3: Creating and viewing a shortlist for comparison
1. User browses the project list on projects.html.
2. On a project card, the user clicks an action such as 'Add to Shortlist' / 'Add to Favourites' / 'Compare'.
3. The page calls addProjectToShortlist with the clicked project’s id.
4. The API returns a shortlist_id and an updated array of project_ids representing the shortlist.
5. The page stores the shortlist_id and project_ids in page-level state and updates any relevant UI indicators (e.g., button state or shortlist count), using the returned project_ids and is_shortlisted flags from subsequent listProjects responses.
6. The user repeats this for additional projects; each time, addProjectToShortlist is called and the returned shortlist_id and project_ids update the page state.
7. When the user clicks 'View Shortlist' / 'Compare Projects', the page navigates to project_shortlist.html, appending the shortlist_id as a URL parameter so the next page can load that shortlist.
8. The user reviews comparisons on the project_shortlist.html page (outside the scope of this page design).

Workflow 4: Preserving and sharing filter state via URL
1. User applies specific filters (project_type, location_region, completion_year_from, completion_year_to) and navigates to a particular page index via pagination.
2. Each filter change or pagination action updates the browser URL query parameters to reflect the current state.
3. The user copies the URL or bookmarks the page.
4. When the same URL is revisited (by the same or another user), the page reads all filter- and page-related URL parameters.
5. The page reinitialises filters and pagination based on those parameters and calls listProjects accordingly.
6. The user sees the same filtered project list and page index as when the URL was created, without requiring any localStorage or session-specific configuration on the client.
interactions: On page load, trigger getProjectFilterOptions, then initialise filter controls and call listProjects using filter and pagination values derived from URL parameters.
When a user changes a filter field (project_type or location_region), update internal filter state and optionally delay the list refresh until the user submits or confirms filter changes.

When a user adjusts the completion year range (e.g., via slider or numeric fields) and confirms the filter changes, normalise the values within the min_year and max_year returned by getProjectFilterOptions, update the URL parameters, reset page to the first page, and call listProjects.

When a user clicks a 'Reset filters' action, clear all filter-specific URL parameters, reset completion year range to the full available range, set page to the first page, and call listProjects with no specific filter values.

When pagination controls are used (e.g., previous, next, or numeric page selection), update the page URL parameter to the selected index and call listProjects with the current filters and new page value.

When a user clicks 'Add to Shortlist' / 'Add to Favourites' / 'Compare' on a project card that is not yet shortlisted, call addProjectToShortlist with that project’s id, then update the in-memory shortlist state using the returned shortlist_id and project_ids and update card-level UI and shortlist actions bar.

When a user clicks the shortlist action on a project card that is already shortlisted (as indicated by is_shortlisted or local shortlist state), either prevent duplicate calls or allow re-adding based on API behaviour, and always sync the UI to the latest is_shortlisted flag from listProjects responses.

When the shortlist state changes (e.g., after addProjectToShortlist), recalculate whether the 'View Shortlist' / 'Compare Projects' button should be enabled and update any displayed count of shortlisted items using the length of the project_ids array from the latest addProjectToShortlist response.

When a user clicks 'View Shortlist' / 'Compare Projects', navigate to project_shortlist.html with the current shortlist_id included as a URL parameter so that the destination page can load the corresponding ProjectShortlist record.

When any navigation occurs that changes URL parameters (filters or page), ensure that back/forward browser navigation correctly updates state by re-reading URL parameters and re-calling listProjects and, if necessary, getProjectFilterOptions.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for filter and pagination state, without relying on localStorage. On initialisation, the page reads project_type, location_region, completion_year_from, completion_year_to, and page from the URL. It then calls getProjectFilterOptions to receive the available project_types, locations, and the global completion_year_range (min_year and max_year). If completion_year_from or completion_year_to are present in the URL, they are clamped to this min/max range; if they are absent, the page sets them to min_year and max_year respectively and may update the URL to reflect those defaults. The current page index is derived from the page parameter; if absent or invalid, a default first-page index is used and may be written back into the URL. Whenever the user changes filters and submits them, the page sets page to the first-page index and rewrites project_type, location_region, completion_year_from, and completion_year_to in the URL query string before calling listProjects with those values plus a configured page_size. Whenever the user changes page using pagination controls, only the page parameter is updated in the URL while the existing filter parameters remain unchanged, and listProjects is called with the new page value and the same filter values. The listProjects response is used to render the projects and read is_shortlisted flags, but shortlist identity (shortlist_id) and list of project_ids are maintained in in-memory state based on addProjectToShortlist responses. When the user clicks 'View Shortlist' / 'Compare Projects', the page navigates to project_shortlist.html, appending the current shortlist_id as a URL parameter so the comparison page can load the corresponding ProjectShortlist entity. Filter and pagination state can be recreated on any reload or external navigation in by re-reading URL parameters, calling getProjectFilterOptions (for valid ranges and options), and then calling listProjects with the derived parameters.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL parameter parsing, initial API calls, state distribution to child components, and updates to URL/query state when filters or pagination change.

Displays and manages filter controls for project_type, location_region, and completion year range, initialising from getProjectFilterOptions and URL parameters, then emitting filter changes back to the controller.
Renders the list/grid of projects returned by listProjects, mapping each project to a card that shows key project fields and an action to add the project to the shortlist.
Provides controls to navigate between pages of results, based on total_results from listProjects and the configured page_size, and updates the page URL parameter upon user interaction.

Shows the current shortlist context (e.g., how many projects are shortlisted based on project_ids from addProjectToShortlist responses) and provides a button to navigate to the shortlist/comparison page.
Detects when listProjects returns no results and displays a functional empty state with actions such as resetting filters to broaden the search.
Tracks loading and error states for getProjectFilterOptions and listProjects calls, showing appropriate loading indicators or error messages and allowing retry actions.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop-oriented grid typical of classic WordPress themes, with the main content constrained to a centered max-width container and internal content (like the 4 images) snapping to equal fractional columns.
layout.layout_pattern: All page-specific components sit inside a single centered main content container beneath the existing global header and dark navigation bar. Within that container, components are stacked in a clear top-to-bottom order, forming distinct horizontal bands: (1) a page header band for the projects-page-controller (title and high-level controls), (2) a compact filters band for the project-filters-bar, (3) the main results band for the projects-list or, alternatively, the projects-empty-state / loading-and-error-handler, and (4) a lower control band that holds the shortlist-actions-bar sitting above the pagination-controls. This mirrors the mockup pattern of logo band → nav band → hero text → image strip → lower text columns.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All page-specific components sit inside a single centered main content container beneath the existing global header and dark navigation bar. Within that container, components are stacked in a clear top-to-bottom order, forming distinct horizontal bands: (1) a page header band for the projects-page-controller (title and high-level controls), (2) a compact filters band for the project-filters-bar, (3) the main results band for the projects-list or, alternatively, the projects-empty-state / loading-and-error-handler, and (4) a lower control band that holds the shortlist-actions-bar sitting above the pagination-controls. This mirrors the mockup pattern of logo band → nav band → hero text → image strip → lower text columns.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content container spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop (a typical max-width of ~1080–1200px), centered with generous left/right margins. Vertically: the projects-page-controller uses a moderate band (about 12–18% of the visible content height), the project-filters-bar is a slim but solid functional band below it, the projects-list consumes the majority of the page (50–60% of the scroll height on initial load), and the shortlist-actions-bar plus pagination-controls form a relatively shallow control band at the bottom. Empty and loading states temporarily take over the projects-list band’s vertical space.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density starts light at the top (page title area with ample breathing room), increases slightly in the filters band with more compact controls, and becomes moderately dense in the projects-list where multiple items appear in a grid similar to the four-image machinery strip. The shortlist-actions-bar and pagination-controls are denser but compact utility areas. Text and controls follow the mockup’s 8px-based spacing: 16–24px vertical padding in blocks, 24–32px separation between major bands, and relatively tight but orderly spacing between repeated items (grid cards, filter fields).
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves from the page title and summary controls (projects-page-controller) at the top-left, over to any secondary controls on the right, then drops downward to the filters band where they scan left-to-right across filter options. From there, gaze flows down into the projects-list, scanning project thumbnails left-to-right in each row (echoing the four-image strip in the mockup) and then downward through subsequent rows. After exploring results, the eye continues down to the shortlist-actions-bar (when visible) and then to the centered pagination-controls at the bottom, before looping back upward if they move to another page or adjust filters.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the site’s existing logo/contact header and dark horizontal navigation bar, the page content sits in a centered, max-width container that feels like a classic business/WordPress theme: flat, functional, and clearly banded. The top of this container opens with a clean page header band for “Projects & Case Studies”, left-aligned and given comfortable vertical breathing room, with subtle supporting controls on the right and a fine horizontal rule along the bottom to anchor the section, much like the mockup’s H1 plus subheading and rule. Immediately below runs a low-profile filter bar spanning the container width, forming a functional strip of dropdowns and search inputs arranged in a single horizontal line that wraps on smaller screens. Under this, the main visual band appears: a grid of project tiles that mimic the mockup’s four-image machinery row—rectangular thumbnails aligned in columns with narrow gutters, allowing many projects to fill the horizontal space efficiently. The grid occupies most of the vertical area, with the main scroll experience happening here. If there are shortlisted items, a compact, lightly highlighted shortlist bar appears just beneath the grid: a horizontal band that calls out the current shortlist count and offers a compare action, but stays visually restrained. Finally, at the bottom of the container, centered pagination controls provide a clear end-stop to the page, separated by a modest gap. When there are no matching projects, a calm, centered empty-state message takes over the grid area; when loading or errors occur, the same band is reused for a simple status display—always preserving the same top-to-bottom structure and overall conservative, content-first feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The projects-page-controller sits at the very top of the main content container, directly beneath the site’s global navigation bar. It spans the full width of the centered container (roughly 70–80% of the viewport width), aligned centrally on the page with generous left and right margins. Internally, the main H1 title “Projects & Case Studies” is left-aligned and positioned at the top-left, with about 24–32px of padding above it and 16–24px below it, echoing the hero text spacing from the mockup. Any supporting elements such as a short descriptor, breadcrumb, or view/sort toggles are arranged to the right side in the same horizontal band: title on the left, concise controls on the right, both aligned vertically to feel like a single, shallow dashboard header. Beneath this content, across the full width of the container, a subtle horizontal rule (1px line in a low-contrast neutral color) runs from left to right, providing the same visual separation that the mockup shows between heading and subsequent imagery. The entire block is relatively shallow in height (around 12–18% of the visible content area on a standard desktop), with about 24px of whitespace separating it from the filter bar below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element in the normal document flow, anchored to the top of the page’s main content area; it does not scroll or stick, but remains the first visible section when the user returns to the top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-filters-bar sits immediately below the projects-page-controller, separated by roughly 16–24px of vertical space to maintain clear banding while keeping the two areas visually connected. It spans the full width of the main content container, forming a low-height horizontal strip that visually echoes a secondary navigation or toolbar. The bar has 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding on both sides. Filter controls (e.g., dropdowns for location/service type, a keyword search, date range) are arranged primarily in a single horizontal row running from left to right; the most important filter (e.g., service type) begins at the far left, with subsequent controls aligned to its right. On narrower widths or when there are many filters, these controls wrap onto a second line while keeping a consistent left alignment, with 8–12px vertical spacing between wrapped rows. On the far right of the bar, any global filter actions such as “Clear filters” or a compact ‘Filter’ button are right-aligned, forming a visual end-cap to the band. Compared to the page title area, the filters bar is slightly more compact and functional, but still maintains at least 16px of vertical margin before the projects-list below so that the page reads as stacked, distinct sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element, extending full container width. It scrolls with the page and remains just above the results; no sticky behavior, in keeping with the conservative theme.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The projects-list occupies the dominant central band of the page, starting approximately 16–24px below the project-filters-bar and extending downward as far as necessary to display the current page’s projects. It fills the full width of the main content container, but internal layout is organized into a multi-column grid that mirrors the mockup’s four-image strip: on desktop, the content is divided into four equal columns (each roughly 25% of the container width minus narrow gutters), with each project tile spanning one column. Horizontal gaps between tiles are modest (about 16px), emphasizing density and efficient use of width. Vertically, rows of projects stack with 24px of spacing between them, keeping the grid structured but not airy. Each project tile is a simple rectangular card: a machinery or site thumbnail image at the top occupying around the upper half of the tile height, followed by the project name, location, and a few key details in concise text. Cards use minimal styling—flat backgrounds, subtle 1px borders, and 16px internal padding—to match the slightly dated, businesslike WordPress aesthetic. On larger screens, the tile grid fills roughly 50–60% of the initial viewport height, inviting scrolling as more rows appear. On narrower screens, the grid responsively collapses to three columns and then two or one, but always preserves the stacked band concept (projects-list remains a single, continuous band between filters and bottom controls). When populated, this section is the main visual and interactive focus of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolling grid occupying the main body of the page. It expands vertically based on content length. When loading, empty, or error states are active, they replace the grid within this same spatial band rather than moving other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls sit below the projects-list, separated by about 24–32px of vertical whitespace so they read as a distinct closing band to the results section. They are horizontally centered within the main content container and occupy roughly 40–60% of its width, ensuring the controls don’t feel stretched edge-to-edge. The pagination items (page numbers, previous/next arrows) are arranged in a single row, with numbers centered and evenly spaced (8–16px between each), forming a compact, symmetrical cluster. A current-page indicator is slightly bolder but still conservative in styling, consistent with the overall flat, content-first design. The vertical footprint of this band is relatively shallow: about 12–16px of vertical padding above and below the controls. Below this band there is an additional 32–48px of bottom padding to provide a comfortable end to the page content before the footer region begins, matching the mockup’s clear separation between major horizontal bands.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline element that always appears at the bottom of the results area. It scrolls with the page and serves as an end anchor; no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shortlist-actions-bar appears conditionally just above the pagination-controls and below the projects-list, occupying a slim horizontal band across the full width of the main content container. When visible, it is separated from the bottom of the projects grid by about 16–24px and from the pagination-controls below by 12–16px, so the three lower bands—results, shortlist controls, and pagination—are clearly segmented. Within the bar, content is arranged in a single row: on the left, a short line of text such as “3 projects selected for comparison” is left-aligned; on the right, one or two primary actions (e.g., a ‘View comparison’ button and a smaller ‘Clear shortlist’ text link) are right-aligned. The bar has a slightly tinted background (a light neutral) and a subtle top border to distinguish it from the white results area without adding heavy decoration. It follows the same 16–24px horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding pattern as other utility bands, making it feel integrated yet subordinate to the main content. If many projects are shortlisted, the bar does not grow taller; instead, the text remains concise so the band stays shallow, preserving the overall conservative, banded layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline within the page flow, appearing only when at least one project is shortlisted. It does not float or stick; as the user scrolls, it moves with the content, always anchored between the projects-list and pagination-controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The projects-empty-state reuses the spatial zone normally occupied by the projects-list. When no projects match the current filters, it appears starting at the same vertical position where the grid would begin—approximately 16–24px below the project-filters-bar—and extends down as a calm, open band. The width is the full main content container, but the content inside is centered both horizontally and vertically within a generous block of space: roughly 64–96px of vertical padding above and below the message. A simple icon or illustration (if used) sits at the top center, followed by a concise headline like “No projects match your filters” and a brief explanatory line, all center-aligned. A primary or secondary button such as “Clear filters” or “Show all projects” sits directly below the text. The layout is intentionally sparse, mirroring the openness of the hero text area from the mockup, to contrast with the otherwise dense grid and help users quickly understand the state. Below this band, the shortlist-actions-bar and pagination-controls are typically hidden or disabled, keeping the empty state as the clear focal band between filters and the bottom whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline replacement for the projects-list; it occupies the same position and width, only appearing when there are zero results. It pushes the pagination and shortlist bars out of view for that state, maintaining a consistent vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-and-error-handler also operates within the spatial area designated for the projects-list, maintaining the same top margin from the filter bar and full container width. During loading states, instead of the grid, a centered message such as “Loading projects…” and a small spinner appear; this loading content is centered horizontally and vertically within a block of 48–80px vertical padding, similar in openness to the empty state but slightly more compact. For error states (e.g., network issues), a small, left-aligned alert-style box appears near the top of the results area, just below the filters: it spans roughly 60–80% of the container width and uses 12–16px internal padding, with concise error text and a retry action. The rest of the space below this alert remains open, so the band doesn’t feel cluttered. In both cases, the handler’s styling is subtle and flat—no heavy modals—so it fits with the conservative theme and maintains the stacked band structure (filters → status band → bottom whitespace or controls). When loading completes successfully, or after a recovered error, this status content is removed and replaced by the normal projects-list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, context-sensitive content that temporarily replaces or precedes the projects-list within the same spatial band. It does not overlay the entire page; it scrolls naturally with the rest of the content and maintains the vertical banded layout.

name: Project Shortlist / Comparison
title: Project Shortlist
description: Page for viewing, validating, and comparing shortlisted construction projects, with options to remove projects and navigate back to the full projects list.

core_features: Load and display the current project shortlist using getProjectShortlistDetails, including project title, type, location (city and region), completion year, and summary.
Show the shortlist identifier returned by getProjectShortlistDetails so users can reference or share the shortlist context.
Display the total number of shortlisted projects and allow the user to specify a desired comparison count, then indicate whether the shortlist matches that count.
Allow users to remove individual projects from the shortlist by calling removeProjectFromShortlist and updating the displayed list.
Allow users to clear the shortlist by iteratively removing all projects via removeProjectFromShortlist.
Provide a stable comparison view that can remain open while users review or discuss shortlisted projects, without automatic navigation away from the page.
Provide navigation back to the full projects listing (projects.html) for adjusting the comparison set by adding more projects.
user_workflows:
1. Open shortlist from projects page:
   1.1 User clicks a 'View Shortlist' or 'Compare Projects' link on projects.html.
   1.2 Browser navigates to project_shortlist.html, optionally including a shortlist_id URL parameter.
   1.3 On load, the page reads shortlist_id from the URL (if present) and calls getProjectShortlistDetails.
   1.4 The page displays the shortlist identifier from the API response and the list of projects returned.
   1.5 The user can keep this page open while discussing or reviewing projects.

2. Validate that a target number of projects is shortlisted:
   2.1 After shortlist data is loaded, the user sees the current total project count.
   2.2 The user enters their desired comparison count (for example, a specific number required by their internal review process) into a numeric input.
   2.3 The page compares the desired count to the actual number of projects from getProjectShortlistDetails.
   2.4 A status indicator tells the user whether the shortlist matches the desired count or shows how many more or fewer projects are currently included.
   2.5 The user can adjust the shortlist by removing projects here or navigating back to projects.html to add more.

3. Remove a single project from the shortlist:
   3.1 In the shortlist table, the user locates a project row.
   3.2 The user clicks a 'Remove from shortlist' control associated with that project.
   3.3 The page calls removeProjectFromShortlist with the project’s id.
   3.4 On successful response, the page either:
       - Uses the returned shortlist_id and project_ids to update local shortlist state, then calls getProjectShortlistDetails to refresh full project details; or
       - Directly calls getProjectShortlistDetails to re-fetch the current shortlist details.
   3.5 The removed project disappears from the comparison table, and the total count and validation status update accordingly.

4. Clear all projects from the shortlist:
   4.1 The user clicks a 'Remove all from shortlist' action when there is at least one project.
   4.2 The page iterates over the currently loaded project ids from getProjectShortlistDetails.projects.
   4.3 For each project id, the page calls removeProjectFromShortlist.
   4.4 After all calls complete, the page calls getProjectShortlistDetails again to refresh the shortlist.
   4.5 The page displays an empty-state message indicating that no projects are currently shortlisted.

5. Adjust comparison set by returning to all projects:
   5.1 At any time, the user clicks a 'Back to all projects' link.
   5.2 The browser navigates to projects.html (no additional parameters required by this page’s design).
   5.3 The user adjusts which projects are shortlisted on projects.html.
   5.4 The user can then reopen project_shortlist.html (possibly in the same or a new tab) to see the updated comparison.

6. Keep comparison view open during discussion:
   6.1 The user opens project_shortlist.html in one browser tab or window.
   6.2 They review project details (title, location, type, completion year, summary) side by side in the table.
   6.3 The user may switch between this tab and other tabs (e.g., email, internal documents) while retaining the shortlist view.
   6.4 If they remove projects during discussion, the view is updated in-place without redirecting away from the page.
interactions:
Page load interaction:
      - Trigger: Initial page load or refresh of project_shortlist.html.
      - Behaviour: Read shortlist_id from URL if present; call getProjectShortlistDetails; set page state with shortlist_id from response and the projects array; derive total count and initial validation state.

Desired comparison count input:
      - Trigger: User changes the numeric field representing desired comparison count.
      - Behaviour: Update internal state desiredComparisonCount; recalculate whether projects.length equals the desired count; update a validation indicator and message accordingly.

Remove project button:
      - Trigger: User clicks 'Remove' on a specific project row.
      - Behaviour: Call removeProjectFromShortlist(projectId) with that project’s id; when the promise resolves, update shortlist_id and project_ids from the response in state (if used) and call getProjectShortlistDetails to re-fetch full project details; re-render the table and validation state.

Remove all projects control:
      - Trigger: User clicks 'Remove all from shortlist'.
      - Behaviour: Loop through all current projects (by id) and call removeProjectFromShortlist for each; after all removals complete (or after the last call resolves), call getProjectShortlistDetails; if the returned projects array is empty, display the empty-state component and reset validation state based on zero projects.

Back to all projects navigation:
      - Trigger: User clicks the 'Back to all projects' link.
      - Behaviour: Navigate the browser to projects.html; no API calls are needed as part of this navigation from the shortlist page.

Manual shortlist refresh:
      - Trigger: User clicks an optional 'Refresh shortlist' control.
      - Behaviour: Call getProjectShortlistDetails again and refresh the projects list and validation state; this allows users to ensure they see any changes that might have occurred from actions on other pages or tabs.

Row expansion or detail toggle (optional, functional):
      - Trigger: User clicks a 'Show more' control on a project row.
      - Behaviour: Toggle a local expanded/collapsed state for that row to show or hide additional text from the project summary (without changing any data via interfaces).
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s primary state is derived from URL parameters and API responses, not from local storage. When project_shortlist.html loads, it reads the shortlist_id query parameter from the URL, if present (e.g., project_shortlist.html?shortlist_id=... ). This URL parameter is used as a contextual identifier for the shortlist (for example, to display it to the user and to keep the browser URL shareable), but all shortlist content is loaded exclusively via getProjectShortlistDetails. The getProjectShortlistDetails response includes a shortlist_id and a projects array; the page treats this response shortlist_id as the authoritative shortlist identifier. If the response shortlist_id differs from the URL parameter, the page can update the URL using the History API (e.g., replaceState) so that the URL always reflects the currently loaded shortlist. After any modifying action (such as removeProjectFromShortlist), the page uses the shortlist_id and project_ids returned by removeProjectFromShortlist only to confirm the shortlist context and then calls getProjectShortlistDetails again to refresh the displayed project details. The current number of projects and the user’s desiredComparisonCount (held in in-memory state) together determine validation status messages, but those values do not affect which projects are loaded. If the page is reloaded or the user navigates back to the URL, the shortlist is reloaded via getProjectShortlistDetails using only the current URL (and any server-side session context), ensuring consistent behaviour across sessions without relying on local storage.

components.functionality: Initialises and refreshes shortlist data for the page by calling getProjectShortlistDetails and exposing the result (shortlist_id and projects array) to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: read URL parameter 'shortlist_id' (if present) and then call getProjectShortlistDetails; store response.shortlist_id as activeShortlistId; store response.projects as shortlistProjects; derive shortlistCount = shortlistProjects.length.
onRefreshShortlist: call getProjectShortlistDetails; overwrite activeShortlistId and shortlistProjects with the response; update shortlistCount.
onShortlistChanged: triggered after any call to removeProjectFromShortlist; calls onRefreshShortlist to ensure the UI reflects the current shortlist.

components.functionality: Displays the page title, active shortlist identifier, total number of projects, and navigation link back to the full projects list.
event_handlers: onRender: read activeShortlistId and shortlistCount from shortlist-data-loader and display them in text fields.
onClickBackToProjects: navigate browser to 'projects.html' when the 'Back to all projects' link is clicked.

components.functionality: Allows the user to specify a desired number of projects for comparison and indicates whether the current shortlist matches that number.
event_handlers: onInit: set desiredComparisonCount to an empty or neutral value; initialise validation state as 'no desired count set'.

onDesiredCountChange: when the numeric input value changes, parse the new value as desiredComparisonCount; compare desiredComparisonCount with shortlistCount from shortlist-data-loader; set validationState to 'match' if equal, or 'under'/'over' if not; compute a message like 'You have X projects shortlisted; your desired number is Y.' based on current values.
onShortlistUpdated: triggered when shortlist-data-loader updates shortlistCount; recompute validationState and message using the current desiredComparisonCount.
